courseMetadata.title: Practical AI for Working Professionals
courseMetadata.description: Practical AI equips working professionals with an accurate mental model of modern artificial intelligence — from conceptual foundations through machine learning and neural networks to large language models — and the hands-on judgment to select, apply, and critically evaluate AI tools in real workflows. The course balances conceptual literacy (how these systems actually work), applied skill (using them well), and critical skill (knowing when not to trust them). Learners move through three acts: building a technical mental model, putting it to work, and learning to audit and verify AI outputs.

prerequisites.description: Comfortable using standard professional software (email, documents, spreadsheets). No programming or statistics background required.
Basic familiarity with at least one AI assistant tool (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, or similar) — having used it at least once, even casually.
Complete the prior knowledge check. Learners who score above 85% on core competencies may be routed directly to advanced sections or transfer-focused assessments.

interleavingGroups.rationaleNote: These three Act-I competencies form the causal chain: what AI is → how systems learn → how networks compute. Interleaving them surfaces the nested relationships (AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL) and prevents learners from treating them as isolated silos. Contrasting supervised learning (ml-fundamentals) with the transformer attention mechanism (neural-network-literacy) during review deepens both.

LLM mechanics is the explanatory engine for tool selection: understanding next-token prediction, context windows, and RAG directly determines how to evaluate a tool's fitness for a task. Interleaving these competencies forces learners to apply mechanistic knowledge to practical decisions — satisfying the llm-mechanics transfer requirement.

Prompting craft is the prerequisite for agentic design: agent instructions are prompts at scale, and poor specification causes goal-drift and compounding errors. Interleaving these immediately after the learner has practised single-turn prompting ensures they carry a calibrated sense of specification failure into their first encounter with agentic architectures.

These Act-III competencies are the course's capstone integration: identifying failure modes (limitation-risk-assessment) motivates the specific verification techniques (content-verification). Interleaving surfaces the mechanism-prediction loop: learners who can explain WHY hallucination occurs can design verification steps to catch it. This group also re-activates llm-mechanics and ml-fundamentals through causal reasoning about error origins.

This cross-act group ensures the course's central payoff: learners who understand LLM generation (Act I) can predict failures (Act III) and know when to stop prompting (Act II). Periodic retrieval items from all three competencies together close the explanatory loop from mechanism to mitigation.

probeQuestions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between artificial intelligence, machine learning, and deep learning?
options: They are three different names for the same technology.
Machine learning is a subset of deep learning, which is a subset of AI.
Deep learning is a subset of machine learning, which is a subset of AI.
AI is a subset of machine learning, which is a subset of deep learning.

optionFeedback.feedback: These terms describe distinct concepts with a specific nesting relationship — conflating them makes it impossible to evaluate vendor AI claims accurately.
This inverts the hierarchy. Deep learning is a subset of ML (one approach among many in ML), not the other way around.
Correct. AI is the broadest category; ML is a data-learning subset of AI; deep learning is a multi-layer neural-network subset of ML. Generative AI sits within deep learning.
This inverts the entire hierarchy. AI is the broadest umbrella; ML and deep learning are progressively narrower subsets within it.

probeQuestions.prompt: A deployed machine learning model is giving outdated recommendations — it doesn't seem to know about events from the past six months. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The model has been learning from user interactions and has drifted toward older patterns.
The model's weights are fixed from its training period; it does not update from new events unless retrained.
The model's context window is too small to hold recent information.
The recommendation algorithm needs more training data added to its database.

optionFeedback.feedback: Most deployed models do not update from user interactions. Weights are frozen after training; any learning from interactions requires a deliberate retraining pipeline.
Correct. Deployed model weights are fixed at the end of the training run. New events after the training cutoff are invisible to the model until it is retrained.
The context window limits what a model can process in one interaction, but it does not cause knowledge staleness. Staleness is caused by the training cutoff, not context size.
ML models learn from training data, not from a live database they query at runtime (unless explicitly built with a retrieval layer). Simply adding data to a database does not update the model.

probeQuestions.prompt: A colleague receives a polished, confident, detailed response from an LLM citing three specific research studies. She concludes the answer is reliable. What critical error is she making?
options: She should have used a larger model for research questions.
LLMs generate statistically plausible text, so a fluent, confident output may be entirely fabricated — fluency does not imply accuracy.
She should have asked the question multiple times to check consistency.
The model's context window may have caused it to lose track of accurate information.

optionFeedback.feedback: Model size reduces some error rates but does not eliminate hallucination. The structural issue — next-token prediction optimising for fluency, not factual accuracy — applies regardless of scale.

Correct. LLMs are trained to produce coherent, fluent text — not to be accurate. A hallucinated citation is indistinguishable in style from a real one. Verification must focus on claims, not tone or confidence.
Checking consistency is a useful probe but doesn't resolve the core issue: all consistent responses may be consistently wrong if the model hallucinated the same fabricated citation repeatedly.
Context window limitations cause the model to forget earlier parts of a long conversation — not to fabricate citations. Fabrication is caused by the generative mechanism, not memory management.

probeQuestions.prompt: You need to use an AI assistant to help draft a document that includes your client's revenue projections, strategic roadmap, and key personnel. Which action is most appropriate?
options: Use the free consumer tier of a major AI assistant — the output quality is the same as enterprise.
Use an enterprise-tier AI tool with a signed data processing agreement that does not train on your inputs.
Use any AI tool but delete the conversation afterward to protect the data.
Ask the AI tool to keep the information confidential before pasting it in.

optionFeedback.feedback: Consumer-tier tools frequently use inputs for model training and offer no contractual data protection. Using them with client confidential data creates legal and compliance exposure regardless of output quality.

Correct. Client financial data, strategic plans, and personnel information require contractual data protections. An enterprise tier with a DPA ensures your data is not used for training and provides the legal basis for processing personal data under GDPR/CCPA.

Deleting a conversation removes it from your view but does not affect how the vendor processes or retains the data on their servers. Data handling is governed by the terms of service, not by whether you delete your copy.

AI tools do not have legal privacy obligations based on conversational instructions — they are governed by their terms of service and data processing agreements. Instructing the model to 'keep this confidential' has no technical or legal effect.

probeQuestions.prompt: You have tried four different prompt formulations asking an LLM to summarise a confidential internal policy document, but all four produce plausible-sounding but inaccurate results. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Try a fifth formulation with stronger emphasis on accuracy.
Switch to a more capable model — larger models make fewer errors.
Provide the actual policy document text in the prompt context, or use a retrieval-augmented system over the document.
Accept that AI cannot summarise policy documents and do it manually.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is a structural failure — the model lacks the document content in its knowledge. Reprompting with different wording produces more confidently worded versions of the same fabrication, not accurate summaries.
A larger model also lacks access to a confidential internal document it was never trained on. Scale does not resolve a knowledge-gap failure; grounding the model in the actual content does.

Correct. The failure is structural: the model lacks the document. The fix is to provide the document text directly in the prompt context (if it fits the context window) or to use a RAG system that retrieves it. Once the model has the actual text, it can summarise accurately.

AI can absolutely summarise policy documents — when given the text. The issue is the knowledge gap, not a categorical incapability. Providing the document resolves the problem without abandoning AI assistance.

probeQuestions.prompt: An agentic AI system is tasked with processing insurance claims: it classifies claims, calculates payouts, and issues payments automatically. After six months of flawless operation, the team removes the human approval step before payment issuance to save time. Why is this a mistake?
options: It is fine to remove the approval step once the system has a proven track record.
Agentic systems improve over time, so fewer approvals are needed as the system matures.
Payment issuance is irreversible; a single novel error after removal of the checkpoint could cause unrecoverable harm regardless of prior performance.
The approval step is only needed for claims above a certain dollar threshold.

optionFeedback.feedback: Prior performance on historical inputs does not guarantee performance on future novel inputs. The approval gate's necessity is determined by the irreversibility of the action, not the system's track record.
Most deployed models do not improve in production — they are frozen at training time and may even degrade as the data distribution shifts. The assumption of ongoing improvement is incorrect.

Correct. Irreversible actions — payments, deletions, sent communications — require human checkpoints regardless of historical performance. Novel inputs with novel errors are unavoidable in production, and the cost of an undetected error at an irreversible step is permanent.

Dollar thresholds can inform the depth of review required, but irreversible actions require at minimum a basic approval gate at any dollar level. The irreversibility property — not the amount — is what determines the checkpoint requirement.

probeQuestions.prompt: A manager receives a market research report she suspects may be AI-generated. She runs it through an AI-detection tool, which returns a verdict of 'likely human-written.' She accepts the report as genuine. What is wrong with this reasoning?
options: Nothing — AI detection tools are reliable enough to use as a final verdict.
She should have run two detection tools and required both to agree before accepting.
AI-detection tools have significant false-negative rates; edited or well-prompted AI text regularly evades them. The verdict is one weak signal, not proof of human authorship.
She should have asked the author directly — self-reporting is more reliable than detection tools.

optionFeedback.feedback: AI-detection tools have documented high false-positive and false-negative rates, degrade against newer models, and produce inconsistent results on the same text. They are not reliable enough to serve as final verdicts in high-stakes decisions.

Running two unreliable tools does not reliably produce one reliable conclusion. Both tools share the same structural limitation — they are always lagging behind current generative models. Agreement between two weak signals is still a weak signal.

Correct. AI-detection tools produce false negatives frequently — edited or well-prompted AI text routinely passes as human-written. The appropriate approach is to treat the detection verdict as one weak signal and combine it with claim verification, provenance review, and stylistic analysis.

Self-reporting introduces its own reliability issues. More importantly, the core problem is not which verification method to use but that no single signal — detection tool or self-report — is sufficient. Claim-level verification is required.

competencies.title: AI Landscape Literacy
competencies.description: Define artificial intelligence precisely, distinguish it from adjacent terms (ML, deep learning, generative AI), and map the major contemporary subfields (NLP, computer vision, speech, recommendation, robotics).

desiredOutcomes.description: Correctly place ML, deep learning, and generative AI as nested subsets within AI and explain the distinguishing feature of each level.
Name and describe all five major AI subfields and give a real-world business application of each.
Identify the three enabling factors (data, compute, transformer architecture) that explain why current AI capabilities emerged when they did.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI means human-like general intelligence (AGI) or robots that can do anything a human can.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Nearly all deployed AI is narrow and task-specific. AGI — machines matching human cognitive flexibility across domains — remains speculative. Confusing narrow AI with AGI leads to misplaced fear and misplaced trust.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Re-expose learner to the nesting diagram and the explicit definition of narrow AI. Provide three concrete examples of highly capable but narrow AI systems (chess engine, spam filter, image classifier) and ask the learner to identify what each cannot do.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI, ML, and deep learning are interchangeable terms.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: They are nested subsets. ML is a subset of AI; deep learning is a subset of ML. Conflating them produces miscommunication when evaluating vendor claims and misallocation of tool trust.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Return to the nesting diagram. Have the learner classify five real products (e.g. expert systems, spam filter, GPT-4, AlexNet, recommendation engine) by which circle they belong in.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI is a recent invention — a product of the last decade.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The field dates to the 1956 Dartmouth conference. What is new is the practical effectiveness of deep learning, driven by data, compute, and the transformer architecture. Understanding this prevents both dismissiveness and catastrophising.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk the learner through the historical timeline: symbolic AI (1950s–80s), ML dominance (1980s–2010), deep learning breakthrough (2012), transformer (2017). Emphasise that 'new' refers to capability, not the field itself.

competencies.title: Machine Learning Fundamentals
competencies.description: Explain how systems learn from data: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning; training vs. inference; features, labels, generalization, and overfitting.

desiredOutcomes.description: Distinguish supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning and match each paradigm to appropriate business problems.
Explain the training/inference distinction and articulate why deployed models do not update from user interactions.
Explain overfitting and distribution mismatch, and predict how biased or unrepresentative training data will degrade model outputs.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Programmers write the rules a machine learning model follows.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: In ML, engineers shape the training data and objective function. The model derives its own statistical patterns — rules emerge from data, not from code written by a human expert.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Contrast a rule-based spam filter (engineer writes every rule) with a trained one (engineer labels emails; model derives patterns). Have learner articulate what the model 'learned' that the engineer never wrote.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: More data always produces a better model.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Data quality, representativeness, and labelling accuracy matter more than volume past a threshold. Scaling biased data amplifies bias. Scaling poorly labelled data scales label noise.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present the Amazon recruiting AI case: 10 years of biased hiring data scaled a biased model. Ask the learner to identify what type of data intervention (quality, diversity of labellers, de-biasing) would have helped versus simply adding more of the same data.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: ML models keep learning and updating from user interactions after deployment.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most deployed models are frozen at training time. Weights do not change during inference. Updating requires deliberate retraining by the model developer, not just user interaction.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Trace the training lifecycle: raw data → training → weights frozen → deployment → inference. Have learner identify which stage allows weight updates (training only) and what is required to add new knowledge to a deployed model.

competencies.title: Neural Network Literacy
competencies.description: Describe how neural networks compute (layers, weights, activation, training via backpropagation at a conceptual level) and distinguish major architectures (feedforward, CNNs, RNNs, transformers) and what each is suited for.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain in plain language how a neural network transforms inputs into outputs through layers of weighted operations, and how training adjusts those weights.
Match CNNs, RNNs, and transformers to their appropriate task families and explain the structural property that makes each suited to its domain.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Neural networks work like the human brain — they are computational replicas of biological neural processes.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The analogy is loose and historical. Artificial neurons are simple weighted-sum math functions, not models of biochemistry, electrical dynamics, or synaptic plasticity. The brain analogy has caused more confusion than clarity in public discourse.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show the actual formula of an artificial neuron (weighted sum + activation function). Ask the learner to identify what this shares with and what it lacks compared to a biological neuron. Emphasise: inspired by, not modelled on.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Bigger and deeper neural networks are always better.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Architecture-task fit, training data quality, and fine-tuning regime drive performance. A smaller model fine-tuned on domain-specific data often outperforms a much larger general model on domain tasks — at a fraction of the cost and latency. Scale brings diminishing returns and compounding costs.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a concrete size-vs-fit example: a 7B-parameter model fine-tuned on legal contracts vs. GPT-4 (much larger) on the same legal task. Ask: what matters more — scale or fit? Surface the cost and latency implications of unnecessary scale.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Neural networks are total black boxes that nobody can inspect or understand.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Interpretability is limited but real. Attention maps show which tokens influenced a prediction. Feature attribution methods identify which input features drove an output. Systematic evaluations (evals) probe behaviour across many inputs. Complete transparency is not achievable, but 'total black box' is inaccurate.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Describe one concrete interpretability technique (attention visualisation) and show what it reveals. Ask the learner to revise their claim from 'total black box' to a more precise statement about what can and cannot be known about a trained network.

competencies.title: LLM & Generative AI Mechanics
competencies.description: Explain what large language models are (next-token prediction over tokens, trained on large corpora), key concepts (context window, embeddings, fine-tuning, RAG), and situate other industrial AI (vision models, diffusion/image generation, speech, recommenders, predictive analytics).

desiredOutcomes.description: Trace the full generation process from input token to output token, naming the roles of embeddings, the attention mechanism, and probability sampling.
Distinguish a base LLM, a fine-tuned LLM, and a RAG-augmented LLM and predict the appropriate architecture for a given knowledge-intensive task.
Apply LLM mechanical understanding to a new AI product or research announcement not covered in the course and correctly classify its architecture and likely capabilities/limitations.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: LLMs look facts up in a database when generating responses.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Base LLMs generate text from statistical patterns in their trained weights. There is no retrieval step — unless a RAG layer is explicitly added. Responses about facts are generated, not retrieved, which is the structural cause of hallucination.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the LLM generation pipeline step by step: token → embedding → attention → probability distribution → sample. Highlight that no database query occurs in this pipeline. Contrast with the explicit retrieval step in a RAG system.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The model learns from my conversations in real time — it updates its knowledge as I correct it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Model weights are fixed during use. Corrections in conversation become context for the current session only. The next user starts with the same frozen model. Real knowledge updates require deliberate retraining by the developer.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Ask the learner to mentally trace a 'correction' through the inference pipeline: the correction appears in the context window but never reaches a weight-update step. Contrast with what would need to happen (gradient computation, backpropagation, weight update) for true learning to occur.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Fluent, detailed, confident output implies the model understood and accurately answered the question.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: LLMs optimise for statistically plausible, coherent text sequences — not for truth. Fluency is the training objective; accuracy is a frequent but not guaranteed by-product. Hallucinated content is typically indistinguishable in style from accurate content.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a hallucinated passage alongside a factually accurate passage on the same topic — both written in identical confident, fluent prose. Ask the learner to identify which signals (if any) distinguish them without external verification. The answer: none that are reliable. Verification must focus on claims.

competencies.title: AI Tool Selection & Workflow Integration
competencies.description: Survey the major AI tool categories in enterprise use (assistants/chatbots, coding tools, meeting/productivity, image/media, analytics, automation), evaluate fit against a task, and design an integration into one's own workflow including data-privacy considerations.

desiredOutcomes.description: Evaluate a candidate AI tool against five criteria (task type fit, data sensitivity, integration needs, cost, HITL design) and produce a reasoned recommendation.
Design an AI-augmented workflow using the ARIA method, specifying which steps AI assists and which remain human, with documented data-privacy rationale.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: There is one best AI tool — just pick the most famous or widely used one.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Fit depends on task type, data sensitivity, integration requirements, and cost. The most famous model may be the wrong choice for a specialised domain task, sensitive data context, or tight budget.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a head-to-head scenario: Task A (general drafting) and Task B (domain-specific Q&A over proprietary documents). Show how the optimal tool choice differs for each. Have learner apply the five evaluation criteria to both.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Adopting AI means automating a job end-to-end — replacing the human entirely.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The highest-value AI integration pattern is human-in-the-loop augmentation of specific steps. Full automation raises error-compounding risk, requires unrealistic reliability, and removes human judgment from the steps that matter most.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through the ARIA Redesign step for a real workflow. Identify mechanical steps (AI accelerates) vs. judgment steps (human owns). Calculate time saved vs. risk added by full automation. The conclusion: augmentation consistently outperforms full replacement in ROI.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Free consumer AI tools are fine for professional work data.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Consumer-tier tools frequently use inputs for model training. They offer no data processing agreements, no GDPR/CCPA compliance basis, and no enterprise-grade security commitments. Using them with client data, financial data, or personal employee data creates legal and compliance exposure.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Read the Terms of Service of a major consumer-tier AI tool together (or a summary). Identify the data training clause and the absence of a DPA. Contrast with an enterprise-tier equivalent. Ask the learner to classify three types of work data (client strategy, personal employee data, published industry data) by which tier is required.

competencies.title: Prompting & Context Craft
competencies.description: Construct effective inputs to generative tools: clear task framing, context and examples, iterative refinement, output-format specification, and decomposing complex work into steps. Critically, recognise when reprompting cannot resolve the underlying problem and select the correct alternative.

desiredOutcomes.description: Construct a prompt incorporating all four components (task framing, context, exemplars, format specification) for a real professional task and demonstrate measurable output improvement over a naive prompt.
Diagnose an underperforming AI output, identify the failure type from the decision tree, and select the correct response (refine prompt, add context, switch tool, or human completion).

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Prompting is about finding secret magic phrases that unlock hidden AI capabilities.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Effective prompting is about clarity, context, and constraints — the same skills as briefing a colleague. There are no incantations; there is only specificity of task, audience, constraints, and format.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Side-by-side comparison: vague prompt vs. structured prompt on the same task. Annotate each added component (task framing, context, format) and show how each addition narrows the output space toward what was intended.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A single perfect prompt should produce the desired output; needing to iterate means the AI failed.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Iterative refinement is the normal expert workflow. Complex tasks rarely fit a single prompt perfectly on the first attempt. Diagnosing output gaps and refining systematically is a professional skill, not a workaround.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a professional prompt-refinement log with three versions, showing how each iteration improved a specific dimension (tone, length, accuracy of framing). Ask the learner to map each change to the component it targeted.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Longer, more detailed prompts are always better.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Irrelevant context dilutes the signal. A 2,000-word prompt packed with tangential background often produces worse results than a 200-word prompt that is precisely targeted. Relevance and structure beat volume.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Run the same core task with (a) a minimal, targeted prompt, (b) a medium-length, structured prompt, (c) a very long, padded prompt. Compare output quality. Have the learner identify at what point additional length began hurting rather than helping.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Reprompting can always fix a bad output — the right phrasing will eventually get what you need.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Some failures are structural. If a model lacks knowledge, capability, or access to required information, no prompt phrasing resolves this. The correct response is retrieval augmentation, a tool switch, or human completion — not a sixth prompt iteration.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present the decision tree: does the model have the knowledge? Is the task within its capability? If no to either: reprompting produces a better-sounding wrong answer, not the right answer. Practise classifying three failure scenarios into 'fixable by reprompting' vs. 'structural failure requiring a different intervention.'

competencies.title: Agentic AI & Automation
competencies.description: Understand AI systems that take sequences of actions (tool use, browsing, code execution, multi-agent coordination) to accomplish goals, identify enterprise use cases, and assess the compounding and irreversibility risks that distinguish agentic from single-turn AI use.

desiredOutcomes.description: Distinguish agentic from single-turn AI across four dimensions (tool use, multi-step planning, memory, autonomous action) and explain the qualitatively different risk profile this introduces.
Assess a proposed agentic workflow for error-compounding and irreversibility risk, and design a minimum-viable HITL architecture with appropriately placed approval gates.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Agentic AI is just a more capable chatbot — the same risks apply, just more of them.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Agency introduces qualitatively new risk categories absent from single-turn use: error compounding across steps, irreversible real-world side effects, prompt injection from external content, and delegation without accountability. These are not scalar increases but new failure modes.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Contrast a single-turn LLM interaction with a 10-step agentic pipeline, step by step. Identify at each step a risk that does not exist in the single-turn model. Build a risk difference table.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: More autonomy is always more valuable — the goal is to remove humans from the loop entirely.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Autonomy saves human time but raises the error-consequence risk. When errors are irreversible, the cost of a single undetected error can exceed the total value saved by automation. The productivity gain from full automation must be weighed against the cost of undetected errors propagating through the pipeline.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Calculate the break-even point for a concrete automation scenario: if an agent processes 500 reports per month autonomously and makes one undetected error every 1,000 runs, what does that one error cost compared to the time saved? Show that the answer determines the correct level of oversight, not the default desire for maximum automation.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Human-in-the-loop approval gates become optional once a system has been tested and proven reliable.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Testing measures performance on historical inputs. Novel inputs with novel error patterns are unavoidable in production. HITL at irreversible actions is required regardless of track record because the consequence of an undetected error at an irreversible step is, by definition, permanent.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a case: system works flawlessly for 10,000 runs, then encounters a novel input class it was never tested on and makes a catastrophic irreversible error. Ask: what would have prevented this? The answer is the checkpoint — not more testing. Formalise: the checkpoint requirement is determined by irreversibility, not by performance history.

competencies.title: Limitation & Risk Assessment
competencies.description: Anticipate and account for failure modes of modern AI — hallucination, training-data bias, knowledge cutoffs, prompt sensitivity, inconsistency, privacy leakage — and decide which tasks warrant AI use, with what safeguards.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain the generative mechanism behind hallucination, training-data bias, and knowledge cutoffs, and predict which task types are most vulnerable to each failure mode.
Classify a given task on the 2×2 risk matrix (error probability × error consequence) and prescribe appropriate safeguards (grounding, review, abstention).
Assess privacy and compliance risks of feeding specific categories of work data into AI tools and prescribe specific mitigations (anonymisation, enterprise tier, DPA, abstention).

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Confident, detailed output is reliable — confidence signals accuracy.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Hallucinations are typically fluent and confident because LLMs are trained to produce plausible, coherent text — not accurate text. Confidence level in the output is not a reliability signal; it is a property of the generation mechanism.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present a hallucinated passage alongside a factually accurate passage on the same topic. Both are written with equal fluency and confidence. Ask the learner to identify any stylistic signals distinguishing them. Conclude: there are none reliable. Verification must be claim-based, not tone-based.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI errors are random glitches that better models or future updates will fix completely.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Hallucination and bias are structural properties of how these systems generate output — emergent from next-token prediction and training data composition. They may decrease in frequency with model improvement but will not disappear. Planning for structural failure modes, not occasional glitches, is the correct risk posture.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Trace hallucination directly back to the next-token prediction mechanism: the model generates the most statistically plausible continuation regardless of factual accuracy. Show that this mechanism does not change with scale — only the frequency of hallucination changes. Distinguish between 'less frequent' and 'structurally eliminated.'

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Safety filters and guardrails ensure AI outputs are unbiased.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Guardrails block specific categories of harmful content (hate speech, violent content). They do not alter the statistical associations embedded in model weights from training data. Everyday outputs on ordinary queries still reflect learned biases that guardrails were not designed to address.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Demonstrate with a bias probe: compare AI responses to the same scenario with demographic characteristics varied. Show that bias manifests in word choice, framing, and emphasis in ordinary outputs — the kind of output that passes all safety filters. Ask the learner to articulate the difference between 'content that is blocked' and 'content that encodes bias subtly.'

competencies.title: AI Content Verification & Provenance
competencies.description: Audit AI-generated content for factual errors and bias (source-checking, cross-verification, bias probes), and assess whether third-party content is AI-generated using stylistic signals, provenance tools, and metadata — while knowing the limits of detection.

desiredOutcomes.description: Perform a complete three-step audit (claim extraction → source-checking → cross-model comparison) on an AI-generated passage and produce a verified, corrected version.
Articulate the structural limits of AI-detection tools and design a multi-signal verification approach that does not rely on them as the primary verdict.
Formulate and document a team verification protocol for AI-assisted work products, calibrating verification depth to content risk level.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI-detection tools give reliable verdicts on whether content is AI-generated.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Detectors have high false-positive and false-negative rates, degrade against newer models, and produce inconsistent results on the same text. They are one weak signal among several — never a definitive verdict. Using them as final arbiter in high-stakes decisions (academic integrity, personnel, publication) is methodologically unsound.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present published false-positive rate data for major detection tools, particularly the elevated rates for non-native English writers. Run the same passage through multiple detectors and show divergent verdicts. Conclude: detectors are calibration instruments, not truth detectors.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: AI-generated text always has obvious stylistic tells that a careful reader can spot.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Modern LLMs produce highly varied, naturalistic prose. Edited or well-prompted AI text is regularly indistinguishable from skilled human writing. Stylistic 'smell tests' produce high false-positive rates (flagging clear human writing as AI) and miss edited AI text. Verification must focus on claims, not vibes.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present three passages — one AI-generated, one human-written, one AI-generated and lightly edited — and ask the learner to classify them by style alone. Reveal the answers. Discuss: what changed when the AI text was edited? What remained? Conclude: style is an unreliable signal; claims are the right unit of analysis.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A careful human read-through reliably catches AI errors, because obvious fabrications would stand out.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Plausible-sounding hallucinations — correctly formatted citations, realistic statistics, credible-sounding expert attributions — survive casual reading. The generation mechanism produces fluent fabrications that are designed (statistically) to be convincing. Only claim-level source-checking catches them systematically.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Read the learner a hallucinated paragraph containing two fabricated citations and one real one — all formatted identically. Ask them to identify the fabrications by reading alone. Then provide the source-check step. Conclude: reading for plausibility is insufficient; checking specific claims against primary sources is necessary.

questions.prompt: A software vendor markets their product as 'AI-powered contract analysis.' When asked how it works, they explain it checks contracts against a database of 500 hand-coded risk rules written by lawyers. Which statement best characterises this system?
options: It is machine learning, because it analyses documents intelligently.
It is AI but not machine learning, because it uses hand-coded rules rather than learned patterns.
It is deep learning, because contract analysis is too complex for simpler AI.
It is not AI, because genuine AI requires statistical learning from data.

optionFeedback.feedback: Machine learning specifically requires systems to derive patterns from data rather than follow hand-coded rules. Intelligent-seeming document analysis implemented via explicit rules is AI, but not ML.

Correct. Rule-based systems that perform tasks requiring intelligence fall under the broad AI category. However, the distinguishing feature of machine learning is learning patterns from data — this system's rules were written by humans, not derived from training examples.

Deep learning requires multi-layer neural networks trained on data. A rule-based lookup system is neither neural nor trained — complexity of the domain does not determine the level of the nesting diagram.

Rule-based expert systems were among the earliest forms of AI (1950s–80s). Statistical learning is not required to qualify as AI — performing tasks normally requiring human intelligence is sufficient.

successCriteria: Identifies the system as AI but not ML, citing hand-coded vs. learned rules as the distinguishing criterion

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies it as AI but not ML and explains the hand-coded vs. learned-pattern distinction clearly.
rubric.adequate: Identifies it correctly but conflates the reason or uses imprecise terminology.
rubric.weak: Misclassifies the system or cannot articulate the rule-based vs. ML distinction.

errorPatterns.description: Learner conflates sophisticated task domain (legal contracts) with advanced AI technique (ML or DL).
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the nesting diagram; emphasise that the technique (rule-based vs. learned) determines placement, not the domain complexity.

errorPatterns.description: Learner denies AI status to rule-based systems, believing AI requires statistical learning.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the historical AI timeline — symbolic AI and expert systems predate ML; AI is defined by task, not technique.

questions.prompt: Which three factors best explain why deep learning became practically powerful around 2012, rather than in the 1980s when the underlying mathematics was already understood?
options: New mathematical discoveries, government investment, and the internet.
Large labelled datasets, affordable GPU compute, and decades of accumulated algorithmic research.
Open-source software, reduced regulatory barriers, and improved processor clock speeds.
The invention of the transformer, cloud computing, and better programming languages.

optionFeedback.feedback: The mathematical foundations (backpropagation, gradient descent) were already established — no fundamental new mathematical discoveries were required. Government investment and the internet contributed indirectly to data availability, but neither alone explains the 2012 breakthrough.

Correct. Three ingredients converged: massive labelled datasets (from the internet), GPU hardware well-suited to parallelising neural network training, and decades of algorithmic improvements that became practical at scale. No single breakthrough — a convergence of scale.

Regulatory barriers and clock speeds were not primary limiting factors. Open-source contributed to diffusion, not to the initial capability breakthrough. GPUs — a form of processor — were critical, but for their parallel architecture, not their clock speed.

The transformer was not introduced until 2017, five years after the 2012 AlexNet breakthrough. Cloud computing and programming languages were enabling infrastructure, not the primary drivers of the capability step-change.

successCriteria: Correctly identifies large datasets, GPU compute, and accumulated algorithms as the three convergent factors

rubric.excellent: Names all three factors and explains the convergence logic — each alone was insufficient.
rubric.adequate: Names two of three factors correctly.
rubric.weak: Cites irrelevant factors or cannot identify the correct combination.

errorPatterns.description: Learner attributes the breakthrough to a single dramatic event (a new discovery, the transformer) rather than a convergence of multiple factors.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the 2012 timeline section; emphasise that each factor (data, compute, algorithms) was insufficient alone and that the breakthrough was the convergence.

questions.prompt: True or False: Because modern large language models can write fluent legal analysis, medical summaries, and financial reports, they qualify as examples of artificial general intelligence (AGI).
options: True — the ability to perform across multiple professional domains defines AGI.
False — today's LLMs are highly capable narrow AI; they excel at text-based tasks but lack the flexible, domain-general cognition that defines AGI.

optionFeedback.feedback: Multi-domain text generation does not constitute AGI. An LLM trained on legal, medical, and financial text generates plausible text in those domains — it does not reason about, learn from, or act in those domains the way a human expert would. AGI requires genuine cognitive flexibility across wholly different domains, not just text-pattern coverage.

Correct. LLMs are narrow AI — extraordinarily capable in text prediction across many domains, but lacking the cross-domain adaptability, embodied understanding, and open-ended learning that AGI implies. The same model that writes a legal brief cannot drive a car, cannot learn a new physical skill, and cannot transfer a reasoning strategy from chess to finance without retraining.

successCriteria: Correctly identifies LLMs as narrow AI and can articulate the gap between current LLM capabilities and the AGI definition

rubric.excellent: Correctly answers False and articulates the specific gap: text-pattern coverage ≠ domain-general cognition.
rubric.adequate: Correctly answers False but provides an incomplete or imprecise explanation.
rubric.weak: Answers True or cannot explain the AGI distinction.

errorPatterns.description: Learner equates multi-domain text capability with general intelligence.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the narrow vs. general AI distinction; provide concrete examples of tasks the most capable LLM cannot perform (physical tasks, true novel learning, real-time adaptation) to sharpen the distinction.

questions.prompt: A board member dismisses a proposed AI initiative: 'AI is hype — this all appeared out of nowhere two years ago and will vanish just as fast.' Which correction is most accurate?
options: AI research is nearly 70 years old; what is recent is the practical effectiveness of deep learning, driven by the convergence of data, compute, and architectural advances — a long buildup with a recent inflection, not a fad.
The board member is essentially right — modern AI began with the launch of consumer chatbots and has no deeper research history.
AI is old, but nothing has changed technically since the 1980s — only the marketing is new.
AI did appear recently, but that is irrelevant because its improvement is guaranteed to continue indefinitely.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. The field dates to the 1956 Dartmouth workshop, with two 'AI winters' along the way. Understanding the long buildup prevents both dismissiveness and catastrophising — the current capability jump is real, but it is the payoff of decades of research meeting sufficient data and compute.
Consumer chatbots are the visible tip of a research programme running since the 1950s — symbolic AI, statistical ML, deep learning, transformers.
The 2012 deep learning inflection and the 2017 transformer architecture are substantive technical changes, not rebranding — they produced qualitatively new capabilities.
Both halves are wrong: the field is old, and future improvement is an open empirical question, not a guarantee.

questions.prompt: A vendor describes their product only as 'AI-powered.' Write the three questions you would ask to locate the system on the nesting diagram (AI ⊃ ML ⊃ deep learning ⊃ generative AI) and to identify its subfield — and for each question, explain what the answer would tell you about the system's likely failure modes.
successCriteria: Includes the rules-vs-learning question (who wrote the rules — engineers or the data?) to separate rule-based AI from ML
Includes a question locating the input/output type (text, images, structured data; classification vs. generation) to identify the subfield
Includes a question about training data provenance or generative vs. discriminative behaviour to place the system in the inner circles
Each question is explicitly linked to a failure-mode implication (e.g. rule-based → brittleness on novel cases; ML → training-data bias and distribution mismatch; generative → hallucination)

rubric.excellent: All three questions are well-chosen and non-overlapping; each is tied to a specific, correct failure-mode implication; the nesting-diagram placement logic is explicit.
rubric.adequate: Questions correctly separate rule-based AI from ML and identify the subfield, but failure-mode implications are generic or only partially connected to the classification.
rubric.weak: Questions do not distinguish the nesting-diagram levels (e.g. all three probe capability or price), or failure modes are not connected to the classification at all.

errorPatterns.description: Learner asks about capability or accuracy instead of classification — treating 'how good is it?' as a substitute for 'what kind of system is it?'
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Revisit the nesting diagram lecture and the route-optimiser worked example: classification questions come first because the system's type predicts its failure modes.

questions.prompt: A music streaming service groups its listeners into clusters (e.g. heavy listeners, weekend casuals, discovery seekers) without using any predefined categories. Which ML paradigm does this represent?
options: Supervised learning — the model is supervised by listener ratings.
Unsupervised learning — the model finds natural groupings in unlabelled data.
Reinforcement learning — the model is rewarded when listeners engage with playlists.
This is not machine learning — clustering is a rule-based technique.

optionFeedback.feedback: Supervised learning requires labelled examples (input paired with a correct answer). Discovering clusters without predefined categories uses no labels — the model creates the groupings without being told what the groups should be.
Correct. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabelled data. Clustering listeners by behavioural similarity without predefined categories is the paradigm case of unsupervised learning.
Reinforcement learning involves sequential decision-making with reward signals. Grouping existing listener data into clusters involves no agent, no sequential actions, and no reward signal.

Clustering algorithms (k-means, hierarchical, DBSCAN) are well-established ML techniques. The absence of labels does not disqualify a technique from being machine learning — it places it in the unsupervised paradigm.

successCriteria: Correctly identifies the paradigm as unsupervised learning and articulates the key distinguishing feature: no predefined labels or categories

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies unsupervised learning and explains the no-labels criterion.
rubric.adequate: Correctly identifies the paradigm but cannot explain what makes it unsupervised.
rubric.weak: Misidentifies the paradigm or cannot distinguish between ML paradigms.

errorPatterns.description: Learner conflates any intelligent-seeming grouping with supervised learning or reinforcement learning.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to paradigm comparison table; ask learner to identify the label and reward signal for supervised and RL respectively, and confirm their absence in this scenario.

errorPatterns.description: Learner denies ML status to techniques without explicit training labels.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the three-paradigm overview; emphasise that ML encompasses learning from data in all three paradigms, including without labels.

questions.prompt: A deployed product recommendation model was trained on user purchase data through December 2023. In March 2025, a product team notices recommendations feel stale and don't reflect a major new product category launched in January 2024. What is the primary reason?
options: The model has been overfitting to old user preferences during deployment.
The model's weights are frozen from its December 2023 training; it has no knowledge of products or patterns that emerged after its training cutoff.
The model's context window does not hold enough interaction history to recognise new patterns.
The model needs more user interaction data to learn the new category through deployment.

optionFeedback.feedback: Overfitting during deployment is not possible — deployed models are frozen and cannot overfit further. Overfitting is a training-time phenomenon. The issue here is knowledge staleness, not overfitting.

Correct. Deployed model weights are fixed at the end of the training run. The model has no mechanism to incorporate information about the new product category without deliberate retraining on data that includes it.
Context windows apply to inference-time input processing in LLMs, not to recommendation model knowledge. The issue here is training-time knowledge, not inference-time context.

Deployed recommendation models do not update their weights from user interactions in real time. Interactions may be logged for a future retraining run, but the live model's weights remain frozen until a deliberate retrain is executed.

successCriteria: Correctly identifies frozen weights and training cutoff as the cause; does not confuse deployment staleness with overfitting or context window limitations

rubric.excellent: Explains training cutoff and frozen weights; articulates that retraining — not user interaction — is the fix.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the correct answer but conflates the explanation slightly.
rubric.weak: Attributes the problem to overfitting or deployment-time learning.

errorPatterns.description: Learner believes the model continues learning or adapting during deployment.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the training lifecycle diagram; trace each stage and highlight which stage allows weight updates and which does not.

questions.prompt: A financial services company adds 10 million more historical loan records to its credit scoring model's training dataset. However, auditors find the model still systematically approves fewer applications from applicants in certain postcodes, despite those applicants being financially equivalent to approved applicants. What does this most likely illustrate?
options: The model needs even more data — 10 million records is insufficient to learn unbiased patterns.
The historical loan data reflects past discriminatory lending practices; adding more of the same biased data amplifies the bias rather than correcting it.
Overfitting — the model has memorised specific postcodes rather than learning general financial creditworthiness.
The training and test sets came from different time periods, causing a distribution mismatch.

optionFeedback.feedback: More data of the same biased composition amplifies bias — it does not correct it. Volume is not a substitute for data quality and representativeness. The problem is the nature of the historical data, not the quantity.

Correct. If historical lending decisions reflected discriminatory practices (e.g. redlining), training data derived from those decisions encodes those practices. Adding more records from the same history scales the bias. The fix requires either re-labelling, synthetic data balancing, or algorithm-level fairness constraints — not more of the same.

Overfitting would show high performance on training data and lower performance on test data. Systematic postcode-based disparity in approvals describes a bias pattern, not a generalisation failure. The model is generalising — just generalising a biased pattern.

Distribution mismatch (train/test split from different periods) could cause accuracy degradation but would not specifically create systematic postcode-based approval disparities. The systematic demographic pattern points to bias in the training labels, not temporal distribution shift.

successCriteria: Identifies the training data as encoding historical bias; explains that volume amplifies rather than corrects bias

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies historical bias encoded in labels; explains amplification mechanism; suggests appropriate remediation direction.
rubric.adequate: Identifies bias in training data but does not explain why adding volume makes it worse.
rubric.weak: Attributes the problem to insufficient data or overfitting.

errorPatterns.description: Learner believes that more data of any kind corrects model problems.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Resurface the Amazon recruiting AI case and the 'biased data scales bias' principle; ask learner to design what type of data intervention would help.

questions.prompt: A lender's approval model is trained on 8 years of historical decisions in which 94% of applications were approved. The trained model achieves 94% accuracy on held-out data. What is the most important caveat before celebrating this result?
options: With 94% class imbalance, a model that predicts 'approve' for every application also scores 94% — the headline accuracy is uninformative, and the historical labels may additionally encode past underwriter bias.
94% accuracy on held-out data is strong evidence the model is production-ready.
The model simply needs more training data to push accuracy above 94%.
The model will improve automatically once it starts processing live applications and learning from outcomes.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. Two distinct data problems hide behind the headline number: class imbalance makes raw accuracy meaningless (evaluate the rejection class specifically), and labels generated by human decisions inherit those decisions' inconsistencies and biases.
Held-out evaluation controls for overfitting, not for class imbalance or label bias. The metric itself is the problem here.
More records of the same 94/6 composition and the same labelling process amplify both problems rather than fixing them.
Deployed models are frozen at training time; they do not learn from live decisions without deliberate retraining.

questions.prompt: Choose an ML system you interact with regularly (spam filter, recommendations, autocomplete, navigation ETA, fraud alerts). Identify: (a) its most likely paradigm; (b) two plausible features it uses; (c) if supervised, what the labels are and who or what generated them; (d) one specific failure you have observed or could predict, traced to a training-data mechanism (biased labels, distribution mismatch, or label noise). Explain the mechanism in (d), not just the symptom.
successCriteria: Paradigm identified with a correct justification (labels present? reward signal? structure discovery?)
Features are concrete and plausibly available to the system (not information it could not observe)
Label source is identified, including who/what generated it and what biases that process could introduce
The failure in (d) is traced causally to a specific data mechanism, not attributed to 'the AI being wrong'

rubric.excellent: All four parts specific and correct; the failure mechanism is traced through the ML lifecycle (data collection → labels → training → deployment) with a plausible causal chain.
rubric.adequate: Paradigm and features correct; label source or failure mechanism identified but the causal chain is incomplete or partly symptomatic.
rubric.weak: Paradigm misidentified, features implausible, or the failure described as random model error with no data mechanism.

errorPatterns.description: Learner describes the failure symptom ('bad recommendations') without a data-level mechanism.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Revisit the 'data is the specification' block and the failure-diagnosis self-guided exercise; require the diagnosis to name a lifecycle stage.

questions.prompt: Which architectural property makes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) particularly suited to image classification tasks?
options: CNNs process input tokens sequentially, which matches how humans read images.
CNNs use sliding filters that detect local spatial patterns regardless of their position in the image.
CNNs are deeper than other architectures, giving them more capacity to memorise images.
CNNs were inspired by the human visual cortex and therefore replicate how humans see.

optionFeedback.feedback: CNNs do not process images sequentially like text — they apply filters across the spatial dimensions simultaneously. Sequential token processing is characteristic of RNNs. Humans do not 'read' images in a fixed sequential scan either.

Correct. Convolutional filters slide across an image, detecting whether a learned local pattern (an edge, a texture, a shape) is present — regardless of where in the image it appears. This translation invariance is what makes CNNs efficient and effective for spatial data.
Depth alone does not explain CNN performance on images — transformers are also deep but do not use convolution. The architecture-task fit (spatial filters for spatial data) is the key property.

The neuroscience inspiration is historical and partial — it guided early design choices but CNNs do not model the visual cortex's full mechanism. Saying a system 'replicates how humans see' overstates the biological analogy.

successCriteria: Identifies translation-invariant local spatial pattern detection via sliding filters as the key CNN property

rubric.excellent: Correctly identifies convolutional filters and translation invariance; explains why this matches the image domain.
rubric.adequate: Identifies the correct answer but cannot explain the mechanism.
rubric.weak: Attributes CNN suitability to depth, brain similarity, or sequential processing.

errorPatterns.description: Learner attributes architecture fitness to depth (bigger = better) rather than structural match to data type.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to architecture comparison; ask learner to predict which architecture would best handle audio and tabular data respectively, and trace the structural property that drives each match.

errorPatterns.description: Learner invokes brain-replication as the explanation for image processing performance.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Revisit the artificial neuron as a math function; contrast with what a biological neuron actually does; clarify that the analogy is loose and historical.

questions.prompt: True or False: Neural networks are complete black boxes — it is impossible to gain any insight into what features or patterns they use to make predictions.
options: True — the billions of weights in a large network make any interpretation completely impossible.

False — while full transparency is not achievable, interpretability techniques such as attention maps, feature attribution, and systematic evaluations provide real, if partial, insight into how networks reason.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is an overstatement. The number of parameters makes exhaustive parameter-by-parameter reading impossible, but interpretability techniques provide real insight: attention visualisation shows which input tokens influenced an output; feature attribution (SHAP, LIME) identifies which inputs drove a prediction. 'Partial but real' is more accurate than 'impossible.'

Correct. Interpretability is limited but genuine. Attention maps show token-level influence on outputs. Feature attribution methods identify which inputs drove predictions. Systematic behavioural evals probe model behaviour across many inputs. The correct characterisation is 'partial transparency' — not 'total opacity.'

successCriteria: Correctly answers False and names at least one real interpretability technique

rubric.excellent: Correctly answers False; names multiple interpretability techniques; articulates the 'limited but real' characterisation accurately.
rubric.adequate: Correctly answers False but vaguely or without naming a specific technique.
rubric.weak: Answers True or equates large parameter count with total opacity.

errorPatterns.description: Learner concludes that large parameter count equals total opacity.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Introduce one concrete interpretability technique (attention map or SHAP) with a real example output; ask learner to describe what it reveals and what it still leaves opaque.

questions.prompt: A regulator asks whether your team can explain what drives a deployed neural network's credit-decision outputs. Which response is most technically accurate?
options: Nothing can be known — neural networks are complete black boxes and no insight into their predictions is possible.

Partial insight is achievable: feature-attribution methods (e.g. SHAP, LIME), attention analysis, and systematic behavioural evaluation can identify what drives predictions — though not a full rule-based readout of the weights.
Full transparency is available — the network's weights can be read back directly as the rules it follows.
Interpretability is unnecessary provided test-set accuracy is high enough.

optionFeedback.feedback: This overstates the opacity. 'Distributed and hard to read' is not 'completely opaque' — real interpretability tools exist and are used in regulated deployments.

Correct. 'Partial transparency' is the accurate characterisation: attribution and behavioural evaluation give genuine, actionable insight even though knowledge is distributed across millions of weights rather than stored as legible rules.
Weights are numerical parameters distributed across the network; they cannot be read back as explicit rules — this is precisely where the interpretability challenge originates.
Accuracy does not answer the regulator's question. A model can be accurate on average while relying on a proxy for a protected characteristic — which only interpretability work would surface.

questions.prompt: Explain to a non-technical colleague, in under 150 words, how a neural network 'learns': what is adjusted, what signal guides the adjustment, and why the resulting knowledge cannot simply be read back as explicit rules. Close with one practical consequence of this for evaluating AI vendor claims.
successCriteria: Identifies weights/parameters as what is adjusted
Identifies the error/loss signal (prediction vs. correct answer) as what guides adjustment, iterated across many examples
Explains that the learned knowledge is distributed across millions of numerical parameters — not stored as legible rules — which is the origin of interpretability limits
Gives a concrete vendor-evaluation consequence (e.g. ask what data it was trained on rather than what rules it follows; demand behavioural evaluation evidence)

rubric.excellent: Accurate, jargon-free, under the word limit; all three mechanism elements present; the vendor consequence follows logically from the mechanism.
rubric.adequate: Mechanism broadly correct but one element missing or imprecise (e.g. no loss signal); consequence present but generic.
rubric.weak: Mechanism described as the network 'being programmed' or 'thinking like a brain'; no distribution-of-knowledge point; no consequence.

errorPatterns.description: Learner leans on the brain analogy as a literal mechanism description.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Revisit Common Error 1 in the network-computation lecture: the brain analogy is historical and loose; the actual mechanism is weighted sums adjusted by gradient descent against a loss signal.

questions.prompt: A legal team is evaluating two AI tools for answering questions about their company's internal contract archive (10,000 proprietary contracts). Tool A is a large general-purpose LLM. Tool B is a RAG system that retrieves relevant contract passages before generating an answer. Which is better suited and why?
options: Tool A — large LLMs have seen enough legal text in training to answer contract questions reliably.
Tool B — because it retrieves the actual contract text and grounds its answers in that evidence, rather than generating from memory.
Tool A — RAG systems are slower and less accurate than direct LLM generation.
Both are equivalent — the quality difference depends only on the model size, not the architecture.

optionFeedback.feedback: A general LLM has never seen your company's proprietary contracts — they are not in its training data. It would generate plausible-sounding but fabricated contract summaries. Training data coverage of 'legal text in general' does not extend to your specific confidential documents.

Correct. RAG retrieves the actual contract text at query time and provides it as context for generation. The model's answer is grounded in the retrieved evidence — reducing hallucination and providing verifiable source citations. For proprietary knowledge-intensive tasks, RAG over the actual documents is the appropriate architecture.

RAG systems do add retrieval latency, but accuracy on knowledge-intensive tasks over proprietary documents is significantly higher than base LLM generation from memory. The speed-accuracy trade-off strongly favours RAG for this use case.

Architecture and access to the relevant knowledge base — not model size — are the primary determinants of performance on proprietary knowledge tasks. A small RAG system over your contracts will significantly outperform a large LLM that has never seen them.

successCriteria: Correctly identifies RAG as the appropriate architecture; explains the retrieval-over-generation mechanism

rubric.excellent: Selects Tool B; explains that proprietary documents were not in the LLM's training data; explains how RAG grounds the response in retrieved evidence.
rubric.adequate: Selects Tool B but only partially explains the mechanism.
rubric.weak: Selects Tool A or believes large LLMs cover arbitrary proprietary documents.

errorPatterns.description: Learner assumes large LLMs have memorised all relevant professional documents.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Return to the training corpus explanation; emphasise that proprietary internal documents are not in public training data. Show the RAG pipeline step-by-step.

errorPatterns.description: Learner equates model size with task-specific fitness.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Surface the architecture-task fit principle; present a small fine-tuned/RAG model outperforming a larger general model on a specific task.

questions.prompt: You correct an LLM's factual error in a chat session. For the rest of that session, the model uses the corrected information. Three days later, you start a new session and ask the same question — the model repeats the original error. What is the best explanation?
options: The model forgot the correction because its context window expired.
The model's weights are fixed during deployment; the correction was temporary context, not a weight update. A new session starts with the same frozen model.
The model was updated between sessions and the correction was lost in the retraining.
Chat history is not retained between sessions for privacy reasons.

optionFeedback.feedback: Context windows are bounded per session, but the deeper issue is that corrections never updated the model's weights. Even if there were an unlimited context window, the next session would still start with the same frozen model — the weights themselves are unchanged.

Correct. Model weights are frozen during deployment. A correction provided in conversation updates the context for that session only — it is not backpropagated into the model's weights. The next session initialises with the same frozen model, and the same factual error is present.

If a deliberate retraining had occurred between sessions, this could explain the loss — but deliberate retraining does not undo a correction from one conversation. More importantly, the scenario describes the original error returning, which is consistent with the frozen-weights explanation, not an intervening retrain.

While chat history retention policies vary by product, the root cause here is not privacy but model mechanics. Even a system that retained all chat history would still start inference from the same frozen weights, with the prior session as context at best.

successCriteria: Correctly explains frozen weights; distinguishes context-window correction (temporary) from weight-update learning (permanent, only via retraining)

rubric.excellent: Explains frozen weights and the distinction between context-window correction and weight-update learning.
rubric.adequate: Correctly selects the answer but cannot fully articulate the weight-update distinction.
rubric.weak: Attributes the error to context window expiry or product privacy policy.

errorPatterns.description: Learner conflates context-window memory (session-level, no weight change) with model learning (requires retraining).
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Trace the correction through the inference pipeline; highlight that context window holds the correction as tokens but no gradient computation or weight update occurs.

questions.prompt: An AI assistant confidently explains a specific regulatory requirement in your industry. The explanation is fluent, well-structured, and cites a plausible-sounding guidance document. Before acting on this, what is the most critical check to perform?
options: Check whether the AI's explanation matches what other AI tools say about the same regulation.
Verify the specific regulatory requirement against the actual official regulatory source, because fluency and confidence do not indicate accuracy.
Ask the AI to rephrase the answer in simpler language to confirm it understood the question.
Check that the AI used the enterprise tier, which produces more accurate regulatory information.

optionFeedback.feedback: Checking other AI tools provides a consistency signal, but multiple models can be consistently wrong on the same hallucinated regulatory detail — especially if they were trained on similar corpora. This is a useful supplementary step, not a sufficient one.

Correct. Regulatory requirements are high-stakes factual claims where LLM errors can have legal and compliance consequences. LLMs optimise for fluent, plausible text — not for regulatory accuracy. The official regulatory source (the actual regulation text, the regulator's guidance) is the only authoritative verification.

Rephrasing tests language comprehension, not factual accuracy. A model can rephrase a regulatory hallucination fluently in five different ways. Verification requires checking the claim against the primary source, not the model's ability to paraphrase.

Enterprise tiers provide data privacy protections — they do not improve factual accuracy for regulatory information. The generation mechanism (and hallucination risk) is the same regardless of pricing tier. Tier selection addresses data handling, not accuracy.

successCriteria: Identifies primary-source regulatory verification as the required action; articulates why LLM fluency and confidence are insufficient signals of accuracy

rubric.excellent: Selects primary source verification; explains that fluency ≠ accuracy and cites the generative mechanism as the reason.
rubric.adequate: Selects the correct action but provides a vague rationale.
rubric.weak: Relies on multi-model consensus or rephrasing as sufficient verification.

errorPatterns.description: Learner treats multi-model consensus as equivalent to primary source verification.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Show a concrete case where multiple models consistently reproduce the same hallucinated regulatory claim (trained on the same incorrect secondary source). Conclude: only the primary source breaks circular corroboration.

questions.prompt: A knowledge-management lead asks why the company's AI chatbot gives different answers to the same internal-policy question on different days — and sometimes cites policy text that does not exist. Which explanation is most accurate?
options: Generation samples probabilistically from a token distribution, so answers vary across runs; and without retrieval grounding, the model generates plausible policy-shaped text from statistical memory rather than from the actual policies. A RAG architecture over the current policy database addresses both problems.
The model is learning from other employees' conversations and updating its answers between sessions.
The model looks policies up in an internal database, which must be corrupted or out of date.
The inconsistency is a bug that the vendor's next model update will eliminate.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. Two structural properties are at work: sampling variability (consistency failure) and ungrounded generation (hallucination). Both are properties of the mechanism — and both are directly mitigated by grounding generation in retrieved policy text.
Deployed model weights are frozen; conversations do not update the model between sessions.
A base LLM has no database lookup step — that is exactly what it lacks and what RAG adds. The fabricated citations come from statistical generation, not a corrupted lookup.
Sampling variability and ungrounded generation are structural, not bugs; updates reduce their frequency but the mechanism remains.

questions.prompt: Trace, step by step, what happens when a colleague asks a base LLM (no retrieval, no web search) for the key findings of a specific internal report the model has never seen. Identify the step at which fabrication becomes likely and explain why the fabricated output is stylistically indistinguishable from an accurate one. State the single architectural change that most directly fixes this, and what residual risk remains even after that change.
successCriteria: Traces the pipeline: tokenisation → embedding/attention → probability distribution → token sampling → iterative generation

Locates the fabrication risk at the probability-distribution step: with no training signal for the specific document, the distribution is anchored to 'what a report summary looks like', not to the report's content
Explains stylistic indistinguishability: fluency, structure, and confidence are properties of the generation mechanism, learned from real summaries, independent of factual grounding
Prescribes providing the document in context or RAG; identifies residual risk (retrieval of wrong passages, misrepresentation of retrieved text)

rubric.excellent: Pipeline traced correctly; fabrication located at the right step with the correct 'plausible-not-accurate' reasoning; fix and residual risk both specific.
rubric.adequate: Fix correctly identified (context/RAG) and fabrication acknowledged, but the mechanism location is vague or the residual risk is omitted.
rubric.weak: Attributes fabrication to model dishonesty or a bug; fix proposed is 'tell it to be accurate' or 'use a bigger model'.

errorPatterns.description: Learner proposes stronger accuracy instructions in the prompt as the fix.
errorPatterns.remediationAction: Revisit the structural failure decision tree: a knowledge gap cannot be fixed by phrasing; the model must be given the information.

assessments.title: AI Tool Selection & Workflow Integration Assessment
