interactionSequence.prompt: Which three factors best explain why deep learning became practically powerful around 2012, rather than the 1980s when the underlying mathematics was already understood?
options: New mathematical discoveries, government research funding, and the rise of the internet.
Large labelled datasets from the internet, affordable GPU compute, and decades of accumulated algorithmic research.
Open-source software frameworks, reduced regulatory barriers, and faster CPU processor speeds.
The invention of the transformer architecture, cloud computing, and the launch of ImageNet.

optionFeedback.feedback: No fundamental new mathematical discoveries were required — backpropagation and gradient descent were understood by the 1980s. Government funding and the internet contributed indirectly to data availability but are not the precise three-factor answer.

Correct. Three ingredients converged: (1) massive labelled datasets made available by the internet, (2) GPU hardware — originally for gaming — proved ideal for the parallel matrix operations in neural network training, (3) decades of accumulated algorithmic improvements (better activation functions, dropout, batch normalisation) became practical at scale. No single breakthrough — a convergence.

Open-source frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch) accelerated diffusion after the breakthrough but were not drivers of the 2012 capability step-change. Regulatory barriers and CPU clock speed were not primary constraints.

The transformer was introduced in 2017 — five years after the 2012 AlexNet breakthrough that launched the deep learning era. ImageNet was the benchmark where the breakthrough was demonstrated, not a cause of it. Cloud computing contributed to scalability but was not one of the three primary convergent factors.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: This three-factor answer (data, compute, algorithms) is worth memorising because it explains the entire trajectory of modern AI. It also predicts the next constraints: if data runs out, if compute gets too expensive, or if algorithmic improvements plateau, progress will slow. Understanding the causes of a capability jump is the best tool for predicting whether the next jump is likely.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: The three factors are: large labelled datasets, GPU compute, and accumulated algorithmic research. The transformer (2017) was a subsequent architectural advance, not a cause of the 2012 breakthrough.
hints: Think about what was scarce in the 1980s that the internet era provided.
GPUs were designed for a completely different purpose — what property made them unexpectedly useful for neural networks?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: True or False: The most capable AI assistants available today are examples of artificial general intelligence (AGI) because they can perform tasks across multiple professional domains.
options: True — performing across multiple professional domains is the defining characteristic of AGI.
False — today's LLMs are highly capable narrow AI; multi-domain text generation is not the same as the flexible, domain-general cognition that defines AGI.

optionFeedback.feedback: Multi-domain text generation is not AGI. An LLM trained on legal, medical, and financial text generates plausible text in those domains because it learned statistical patterns — it does not reason, plan, or learn from novel situations the way a human expert would. AGI requires genuine cognitive flexibility across wholly different domains, not pattern coverage in text.

Correct. Today's LLMs are narrow AI — extraordinarily capable at text prediction across many domains, but lacking the cross-domain adaptability, open-ended learning, and physical grounding that AGI implies. The same model that writes a legal brief cannot drive a car, cannot learn a new skill from three examples the way a child can, and cannot transfer a strategy it learned in one context to a wholly unrelated one without retraining.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The AGI misconception is consequential in both directions: it leads some people to over-trust AI outputs (if it's general intelligence, it must know things reliably) and others to dismiss near-term risks (if it's not really general, it's not really dangerous). The accurate position is that today's AI is narrow but remarkably capable within its operational envelope — and understanding that envelope precisely is what this course is about.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: False. Multi-domain text capability is not AGI. Narrow AI can cover many domains in text form; what it lacks is domain-general cognition, the ability to learn genuinely new skills, and physical embodiment.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: Rank these four descriptions from the BROADEST AI category (1) to the NARROWEST (4). Drag to reorder.
options: A system that generates realistic images from text prompts (e.g. Midjourney)
A neural network trained on 100 billion text tokens to predict the next word
A rule-based expert system that diagnoses equipment faults using 200 engineering rules
A spam classifier that learned to distinguish spam from legitimate email by training on labelled examples

optionFeedback.feedback: This is Generative AI — the innermost circle. It is a deep learning model (therefore also ML and AI) that specifically generates new content from text prompts. Narrowest category.

This is Deep Learning — second-narrowest. It is a neural network with many layers (therefore ML and AI), trained to predict tokens. It is not specifically generative unless the generation objective is the training target.
This is AI but not ML — the broadest category here. It performs an intelligent task (fault diagnosis) using hand-coded rules, not learned patterns. AI without ML is the outermost circle.

This is Machine Learning — second-broadest. The system learned patterns from labelled data. It is likely not deep learning (a spam classifier typically uses classical ML) and not generative (it classifies rather than creates).

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Correct order broadest to narrowest: Rule-based expert system (AI only) → Spam classifier trained on examples (ML, not deep learning) → Next-token prediction neural network (deep learning) → Image generator (generative AI). Notice that each narrower category is also a member of all broader categories: a generative AI model is also deep learning, also ML, also AI.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Broadest to narrowest: Rule-based expert system → ML spam classifier → Deep learning language model → Generative image model. Each inner circle is also a member of all outer circles.
hints: Start by asking: does each system learn from data at all? If not, it belongs in the outermost AI circle only.
Among those that learn from data, does the system generate new content or classify/predict existing categories?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this ranking? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: Match each AI subfield to a business application you might encounter in your own sector. For each, write one sentence: (a) NLP in my sector could be used for ___. (b) Computer vision in my sector could be used for ___. (c) Predictive analytics in my sector could be used for ___.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: This reflection is the foundation of Section 4, where you will design an AI-augmented workflow for your own role. The more specific you are now — naming a real task, a real document type, a real decision — the more useful that exercise will be. Return to these notes when you reach Section 4.

workedExamples.problem: A logistics company is evaluating a vendor pitch for an 'AI-powered route optimiser' that promises a 15% reduction in fuel costs. You need to determine what type of AI system is actually involved and what questions to ask before trusting the claim.
steps: Step 1 — Identify the task type. Route optimisation takes inputs (delivery locations, vehicle capacity, traffic data, time windows) and produces an output (a sequence of stops). This is a prediction or decision task, not a content-generation task. It falls outside generative AI.

Step 2 — Ask the foundational question: rules or learning? 'Did human engineers write the optimisation logic, or did the system learn it from data?' If the vendor describes decision trees and manually coded heuristics — this is AI but not ML. If they describe training on historical route and outcome data — this is ML.

Step 3 — Identify the subfield. Route optimisation using geographic and traffic data is a predictive analytics or operations research problem. It does not primarily involve language (NLP) or images (computer vision).

Step 4 — Interrogate the 15% claim. Ask: 'What was the baseline? Over what time period? On what route network? How was the model trained, and on whose data?' A ML model trained on urban US routes may perform poorly on rural European networks. The specific claim requires specific validation data.

Step 5 — Assess the deployment question. Ask: 'Is the model retrained as traffic patterns change, or is it frozen from the original training?' A model frozen to 2022 traffic patterns will degrade as conditions evolve.
workedExamples.conclusion: A five-question interrogation using the nesting diagram and subfield vocabulary surfaces the critical evaluation criteria that a simple vendor demo never would. The 15% claim becomes credible or suspicious depending on the answers — and you now have the vocabulary to ask the right questions.

retrievalPractice.prompt: Without looking at your notes: draw the nesting diagram (AI ⊃ ML ⊃ Deep Learning ⊃ Generative AI) and write one sentence explaining the distinguishing feature of each level.
Name the five major AI subfields covered in this section. For each, give one real business application. (Hint: NLP, Computer Vision, Speech, Recommendation, and one more.)

A colleague says: 'Our new AI system automatically flags high-risk contracts by checking for 12 specific clause types we defined.' Is this ML? Justify your answer using the distinguishing criterion from the nesting diagram.
What three factors converged around 2012 to make deep learning practically powerful? (One-word hints: data, _____, _____)
What is the difference between AGI (artificial general intelligence) and the AI systems deployed commercially today? Why does this distinction matter for how much you should trust an AI output?

transferExercises.scenario: A hospital's administration team is evaluating two AI products pitched at the same conference. Product A: 'Our AI analyses clinician notes and automatically codes each patient visit to the correct ICD-10 billing code using natural language understanding trained on 50 million records.' Product B: 'Our AI ensures billing accuracy by enforcing 300 compliance rules written by our certified coding experts, flagging any visit that violates these rules.'
transferExercises.task: For each product: (1) identify which circle of the nesting diagram it occupies, (2) name the AI subfield, (3) identify one failure mode specific to that type of system, and (4) formulate one question you would ask the vendor before trusting the system's output.
successCriteria: Product A correctly identified as ML (learned from labelled records) in the NLP subfield.
Product B correctly identified as AI-but-not-ML (hand-coded rules), in a rule-based/expert-system category.
Product A failure mode references distribution mismatch, hallucination risk, or training data bias.
Product B failure mode references brittleness to novel billing codes or edge cases not covered by the 300 rules.
Both questions target the specific limitation of the respective system type.

transferExercises.scenario: A digital news organisation uses three AI tools: (1) a tool that analyses reader comments for toxicity and hides borderline ones using patterns learned from 2 million moderated examples; (2) a tool that recommends the next article a reader should read based on their reading history and the behaviour of similar readers; (3) a tool that transcribes audio interviews into text in real time.
transferExercises.task: For each tool: identify the AI subfield and state whether it is ML or rule-based. Then identify one plausible way each tool could fail specific to your classification.
successCriteria: Tool 1 identified as ML (learned from moderated examples) in NLP/content moderation — failure mode: bias in the moderation training data or new types of toxicity not in training.
Tool 2 identified as ML recommendation system — failure mode: filter bubble, cold-start problem for new readers, or amplification of sensational content.
Tool 3 identified as ML speech AI — failure mode: accuracy degradation on accented speech, overlapping voices, or noisy environments.

learningExperiences.title: Practical Activity: Subfield Taxonomy of Your Own Work

contentBlocks.content:
PRACTICAL ACTIVITY: Subfield Taxonomy of Your Own Work

This activity builds the seed for your Section 4 workflow design. You need 20–30 minutes and do not need any external tools.

Step 1 — List five tasks in your current role that are repetitive, data-intensive, or involve recognising patterns. Be specific: not 'reporting' but 'pulling weekly sales numbers from three systems and writing a narrative for the regional manager.'

Step 2 — For each task, identify:
  • What type of input data is involved (text, numbers, images, audio, video, mixed)?
  • What type of output is needed (classification, prediction, generation, search, summarisation)?
  • Which AI subfield best matches the task (NLP, computer vision, speech, recommenders, predictive analytics, generative AI)?

Step 3 — For each task you mapped to an AI subfield, find one real-world example of an organisation already using AI for that task type. A 10-minute search is sufficient — you are looking for existence proofs, not deep case studies.

Deliverable: A simple five-row table — Task | Input Type | Output Type | AI Subfield | Real-World Precedent.

Save this table. It becomes the starting inventory for the ARIA workflow design exercise in Section 4.

interactionSequence.prompt: After completing the table: which of your five tasks surprised you most — either because it mapped more cleanly to an AI subfield than you expected, or because you struggled to map it at all? Write two to three sentences on what that difficulty (or ease) tells you about the task.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Tasks that resist clean subfield mapping often involve either (a) judgment-intensive decisions where human context is irreplaceable, or (b) multi-modal inputs (text + numbers + relationship knowledge) that span several subfields simultaneously. Both are important signal for the ARIA design work in Section 4: type (a) tasks should stay human; type (b) tasks may benefit from a multi-tool approach.

learningExperiences.title: Section Quiz: AI Landscape

contentBlocks.content:
SECTION QUIZ

Answer all questions before viewing feedback. Confidence ratings are recorded and used to calibrate your retrieval schedule — higher confidence on a wrong answer flags a deeply held misconception and triggers additional review.

interactionSequence.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between artificial intelligence, machine learning, and deep learning?
options: They are three different names for the same technology.
Machine learning is a subset of deep learning, which is a subset of AI.
Deep learning is a subset of machine learning, which is a subset of AI.
AI is a subset of machine learning, which is a subset of deep learning.

optionFeedback.feedback: These three terms describe distinct concepts with a specific nesting relationship. Treating them as synonyms makes it impossible to evaluate vendor AI claims accurately — a core skill this course develops.

This inverts the hierarchy. Deep learning is one approach within ML (one of many), not the other way around. ML encompasses many techniques beyond deep learning: decision trees, SVMs, random forests, and more.

Correct. AI is the broadest category (systems performing intelligent tasks). ML is a subset (systems that learn from data). Deep learning is a subset of ML (multi-layer neural networks). Generative AI sits within deep learning.
This inverts the entire hierarchy. AI is the broadest umbrella; all other terms are progressively narrower subsets within it.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: The correct hierarchy: AI (broadest) ⊃ ML ⊃ Deep Learning ⊃ Generative AI (narrowest).

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A retail company uses AI to predict which customers are likely to stop purchasing in the next 90 days, enabling the sales team to reach out proactively. Which AI subfield best describes this application?
options: Computer vision
Natural language processing
Predictive analytics
Generative AI

optionFeedback.feedback: Computer vision processes image and video data. Customer churn prediction uses structured transactional and behavioural data — no images involved.
NLP works with text and speech. While customer communications might involve NLP, churn prediction from purchase history, login frequency, and plan tier is a structured-data prediction task.

Correct. Predicting future outcomes (churn, demand, equipment failure) from historical structured data is predictive analytics — one of the highest-value and most mature AI applications in enterprise settings.
Generative AI creates new content. Churn prediction is a classification or regression task: will this customer leave? This is forecasting, not generation.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Predicting outcomes from historical structured data = predictive analytics.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A logistics company's 'AI route optimiser' was built in 2003 using hand-coded traffic rules and seasonal adjustment tables. Which statement best characterises this system?
options: It is AI but not machine learning.
It is machine learning but not deep learning.
It is generative AI applied to logistics.
It is not AI at all, because it was built before modern AI techniques existed.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. Rule-based systems that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence fall within the broad AI category, even without machine learning. This system optimises routes — a cognitively demanding task — through explicitly coded rules rather than learned patterns.
Machine learning requires learning patterns from data. Hand-coded rules written by engineers is AI without ML, not ML without deep learning.
Generative AI creates new content. A route optimiser makes decisions about sequences and timing — it is a planning or optimisation task, not content generation.

Rule-based expert systems are among the oldest and most established forms of AI — deployed commercially since the 1970s. 'Modern techniques' are not a prerequisite for AI status. The field predates ML, deep learning, and transformers by decades.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Hand-coded expert systems = AI but not ML. The 2003 build date is irrelevant — AI predates modern ML by decades.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A vendor pitch claims: 'Our assistant is built on an AGI-class model — it can draft contracts, analyse spreadsheets, and write marketing copy.' What is the most accurate assessment of this claim?
options: The claim is justified — performing well across several professional domains is the definition of AGI.

The claim is marketing overreach — multi-domain text generation is still narrow AI; AGI refers to flexible, human-like general cognition across arbitrary domains, which no deployed system has demonstrated.
The claim is understated — modern assistants already exceed AGI within text-based tasks.
The claim cannot be evaluated without knowing the model's parameter count.

optionFeedback.feedback: Breadth across text-based professional tasks is impressive but is not domain-general cognition. All of the listed capabilities are variations of one underlying task: generating text.
Correct. Today's systems are highly capable narrow AI. Treating 'AGI' as a marketing label rather than a technical claim leads to both misplaced trust and unrealistic expectations.
'Exceeding AGI' within one modality is a contradiction — generality across domains is the defining property of AGI, not peak performance within one.
Parameter count measures scale, not generality. A larger narrow model is still a narrow model.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: False. Today's LLMs are narrow AI — not AGI. AGI remains a research target, not a deployed reality.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A colleague argues: 'Deep learning must have come from a single new mathematical discovery around 2012 — nothing changes a field that fast otherwise.' Which correction is most accurate?
options: They are essentially right — backpropagation was discovered in 2012, which is what made deep networks trainable.

The core mathematics had existed for decades; what changed around 2012 was the convergence of internet-scale labelled data, affordable GPU compute, and accumulated algorithmic refinement — making long-standing ideas practical at scale.
The change was driven primarily by the 2017 transformer paper, which introduced the first practical deep learning architecture.
The shift happened because government agencies declassified neural network research that had been restricted since the 1970s.

optionFeedback.feedback: Backpropagation dates to research from the 1970s–80s, decades before 2012. The 2012 moment was about the conditions for applying it at scale, not its invention.
Correct. AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet result was a discontinuous jump produced by converging ingredients — data, compute, and matured algorithms — not a single new discovery.
The transformer (2017) came five years after the deep learning inflection point and built on it; CNNs drove the 2012 breakthrough.
There was no declassification event — this narrative has no basis. The enabling factors were data, compute, and algorithmic maturity.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: The three convergent factors: large labelled datasets, GPU compute, accumulated algorithmic research. The transformer came later (2017).

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

sections.title: How Machines Learn: ML Fundamentals
sections.description: Builds the core mental model of learning from data — the three ML paradigms, how training and inference differ, the roles of features and labels, and why data quality and representativeness determine model quality. Plants the first seed of limitation-risk-assessment by connecting overfitting and data bias to real deployment failures. Reinforces the Section 1 nesting diagram throughout.

learningObjectives.description: Differentiate supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, explain the distinguishing feature of each, and match each paradigm to at least two realistic business problems.
Explain the training/inference distinction, describe what happens to model weights at each stage, and articulate why deployed models do not update from user interactions.
Explain generalisation, overfitting, and distribution mismatch, and predict how biased or unrepresentative training data will degrade model outputs in a given scenario.

motivationHooks.prompt: Before reading: a fraud detection model was trained on 50 million transactions and achieves 99.2% accuracy on its training data. When deployed, it flags legitimate purchases as fraud at twice the expected rate for customers in certain zip codes. Predict: what went wrong? Write one sentence before reading on. You will revisit your prediction after the lecture.

In 2018, Amazon scrapped an AI recruiting tool after discovering it systematically downgraded résumés containing the word 'women's' — because it had learned from 10 years of historically male-skewed hiring decisions. More data did not fix the problem. Understanding why is the central lesson of this section.

Every AI tool you will evaluate in this course — chatbots, coding assistants, analytics platforms — was built using one of three learning paradigms. Understanding which paradigm underlies a tool tells you what kind of failure to expect, what data it needed to work, and whether it can update after deployment. These are the questions that separate informed AI users from credulous ones.

furtherReading.title: A Few Useful Things to Know About Machine Learning
furtherReading.author: Pedro Domingos
furtherReading.rationaleNote: A highly readable 12-page paper covering the practical pitfalls of ML — overfitting, the curse of dimensionality, and the importance of features. Requires no mathematics and directly reinforces the overfitting and generalisation concepts from this section.

furtherReading.title: Machine Learning Crash Course
furtherReading.author: Google
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Google's free practical ML course. The first three modules (Framing, Descending into ML, Reducing Loss) extend Section 2's concepts with interactive visualisations. Recommended for learners who want a more hands-on follow-up without requiring programming.

furtherReading.title: Weapons of Math Destruction
furtherReading.citation: O'Neil, C. (2016). Weapons of Math Destruction: How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy. Crown.
furtherReading.author: Cathy O'Neil
furtherReading.rationaleNote: A practitioner-accessible account of how biased training data propagates harm through deployed ML systems. Directly extends the Amazon recruiting AI case and the data-quality thread introduced in Learning Objective 3. Essential reading for anyone deploying ML in high-stakes contexts.

learningExperiences.title: Lecture: Rules vs. Learning — The Big Idea

contentBlocks.content:
WHY THIS MATTERS

The single most powerful idea in modern AI is that machines can acquire useful behaviours by learning from examples rather than following explicit instructions. This sounds obvious once stated, but it has a counterintuitive implication: the behaviour of an ML system is not designed — it emerges from data. That means the data decides what the model does. This is both why ML is powerful and why it can fail in ways that are hard to anticipate.

If you understand how learning from data works — and where it breaks down — you will be able to predict failure modes before they occur. That is the payoff of this section and the foundation of everything in Act III of this course.

KEY TERMS & DEFINITIONS

Supervised Learning: ML paradigm where the model is trained on labelled examples — each input paired with the correct answer. The model learns to predict the answer for new inputs.

Unsupervised Learning: ML paradigm where the model finds structure in unlabelled data — no correct answers are provided. Used to discover natural groupings or compressed representations.

Reinforcement Learning (RL): ML paradigm where an agent learns by taking actions, receiving reward or penalty signals, and adjusting its behaviour to maximise cumulative reward over time.

Training: The process of adjusting a model's parameters on a dataset to minimise prediction error. Weights change only during training.

Inference: Using a trained, fixed model to generate predictions on new data. Weights do not change during inference.

Feature: An individual measurable input property used by a model (e.g. 'number of days since last purchase', 'transaction amount', 'hour of day').

Label: The correct answer associated with a training example (e.g. 'fraud' or 'not fraud'). In supervised learning, the model learns to predict labels from features.

Generalisation: The ability of a trained model to perform well on new, unseen data — not just on the data it was trained on. The primary goal of ML model development.

Overfitting: When a model learns training data too precisely — including its noise and idiosyncrasies — and fails to generalise to new data. Indicated by high training accuracy but poor test accuracy.

Training set: Data used to fit the model's parameters.

Test set: Data held out and never seen during training; used only to evaluate the trained model.

Distribution mismatch: When training data and deployment data come from different populations or time periods, causing the model's learned patterns to transfer poorly.

THE BIG IDEA: RULES vs. LEARNING

Classical programming works like this: a human expert identifies the logic needed to solve a problem, writes that logic as code, and the computer executes it. The intelligence lives in the programmer.

Machine learning inverts this: a human provides many examples of inputs paired with correct outputs, an algorithm finds the pattern mapping inputs to outputs, and that pattern becomes the program. The intelligence emerges from the data.

This inversion has profound consequences:

1. The model can discover patterns no human thought to encode. A fraud detection model trained on 50 million transactions may identify a combination of merchant category, transaction time, geographic distance from home, and purchase velocity that no analyst ever specified — yet which proves highly predictive.

2. The model can only learn what is present in its features and labels. If a crucial factor (say, a customer's stated dissatisfaction with a product) was never recorded as a feature, the model cannot use it — even if a human expert knows it matters.

3. The model reflects the biases in its training data. If the labelled outcomes were generated by a biased process — biased human annotators, historically discriminatory decisions, non-representative samples — the model learns to replicate and potentially amplify that bias.

COMMON ERRORS

Error 1 — 'Programmers write the rules the model follows.'
In ML, engineers design the training process and define the objective function. The model derives its own statistical patterns from data. Those patterns are not legible rules you can read back. They are distributed across millions or billions of numerical parameters. This is where 'black box' concerns originate — and we will address the nuance of interpretability in Section 3.

Error 2 — 'More data always produces a better model.'
Data quality, representativeness, and labelling accuracy matter more than volume past a threshold. Adding 10 million more records of the same biased composition scales the bias — it does not correct it. The Amazon recruiting AI case is the paradigm example: 10 years of biased hiring data produced a model that learned to replicate that bias with greater efficiency.

Error 3 — 'ML models keep learning from user interactions after deployment.'
Most deployed models are frozen at training time. When you correct an LLM's factual error in a chat session, that correction lives in the context of that session only — it does not update the model's weights. Updating a model's underlying knowledge requires deliberate retraining by the model developer, not conversation.

interactionSequence.prompt: Revisit your prediction from the motivation hook: a fraud model achieves 99.2% training accuracy but flags legitimate purchases at twice the expected rate for customers in certain zip codes. Now that you have read about data bias and distribution mismatch — what is your revised diagnosis? Write two to three sentences.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The most likely diagnosis: the training data over-represented certain transaction patterns associated with specific zip codes — either because fraud was historically concentrated there (amplifying geographic bias) or because legitimate transactions in those areas were disproportionately flagged by prior human reviewers (scaling a human error). High training accuracy means the model learned the patterns in the training data very well. The deployment failures show those patterns do not transfer to all zip codes equally. This is simultaneously overfitting (to a non-representative sample) and distribution mismatch. No amount of prompting or instruction can fix this — the data pipeline is where the fix must happen.

interactionSequence.prompt: A streaming platform builds a tool that groups its 80 million listeners into behavioural clusters — 'discovery-driven,' 'mood-based,' 'catalogue completionists' — without using any predefined categories. Which ML paradigm does this represent?
options: Supervised learning — the model is learning the correct cluster for each listener.
Unsupervised learning — the model discovers natural structure in unlabelled listener data.
Reinforcement learning — the model is rewarded when listeners engage with recommended content.
This is not machine learning — clustering uses rule-based logic, not learning.

optionFeedback.feedback: Supervised learning requires labelled examples — each input paired with a predefined correct answer. There are no predefined correct cluster assignments here. The model creates its own groupings without being told what the clusters should be.

Correct. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabelled data. Discovering listener clusters without predefined categories is its paradigm use case. The model identifies natural groupings based on behavioural similarity — no human labelled any listener as a 'discovery-driven' listener in advance.

Reinforcement learning involves an agent taking sequential actions and receiving reward signals over time. Grouping existing static listener data into clusters involves no sequential decision-making and no reward signal.
Clustering algorithms (k-means, hierarchical clustering, DBSCAN) are established ML techniques. The absence of human-provided labels places this in unsupervised learning — not outside ML altogether.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The key diagnostic question for paradigm classification: Was a correct answer provided for each training example? If yes → supervised. If no, but the agent receives reward signals for actions taken → reinforcement. If no correct answers and no rewards — just finding structure in raw data → unsupervised.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Unsupervised learning: no predefined labels, no rewards — just finding structure in raw data.
hints: Ask: did any human label any listener with a cluster category before training began?
If no human provided correct answers, you are not in supervised territory.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: True or False: When you correct an LLM's factual mistake in a chat conversation, this updates the model's knowledge so the same error will not appear for other users.
options: True — the model learns from corrections in real time to improve future responses.
False — deployed model weights are frozen; your correction lives in the context of your session only and does not alter the underlying model.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is one of the most consequential misconceptions about deployed AI. Weights are fixed after training. Your correction is processed as context tokens — it influences the rest of your session but triggers no weight-update mechanism. The next user encounters the same frozen model with the same underlying knowledge state.

Correct. The training/inference distinction is fundamental: weights change only during training. Updating a model's knowledge requires the developer to run a new training cycle — an expensive, deliberate process that typically happens on scheduled intervals, not in response to individual chat corrections.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: This distinction becomes essential in Section 5 when you learn to diagnose whether an AI output failure can be fixed by reprompting (within-session context) or whether it is a structural knowledge gap requiring a different solution — retrieval augmentation, a tool switch, or human expertise. Knowing that corrections do not persist is the first step in that diagnosis.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: False. Deployed weights are frozen. Chat corrections are temporary context, not permanent learning.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

learningExperiences.title: Lecture: The Three Paradigms in Depth

contentBlocks.content:
THE THREE ML PARADIGMS IN DEPTH

SUPERVISED LEARNING — Learning from labelled examples

How it works: The model is shown pairs of (input, correct answer). It makes a prediction, computes the gap between its prediction and the correct answer (the loss), adjusts its internal parameters to reduce that gap, and repeats across millions of examples. Over many iterations, parameters settle into values that produce accurate predictions on data resembling the training set.

When to use it: When you have historical data where you already know the correct answers, and you want to predict the answer for new cases.

Business examples:
• Email spam detection (labels: spam / not spam)
• Customer churn prediction (labels: churned / retained)
• Loan default prediction (labels: defaulted / repaid)
• Medical image diagnosis (labels: disease present / absent)
• Sentiment analysis of product reviews (labels: positive / negative / neutral)

Key constraint: You need labelled data. Labelling is expensive. A hospital wanting to train a tumour detection model needs radiologists to label thousands of scans. A law firm wanting to classify contract clauses needs lawyers to review thousands of contracts. Label scarcity is one of the main practical bottlenecks in real-world ML deployment.

---

UNSUPERVISED LEARNING — Finding structure without labels

How it works: The model receives input data only — no correct answers. It must find natural groupings, patterns, or compressed representations in the data on its own.

When to use it: When you want to discover structure you did not know to look for, or when labelling data at scale is infeasible.

Business examples:
• Customer segmentation (group customers by behaviour without predefining the groups)
• Anomaly detection in network traffic (flag unusual patterns without prelabelling attacks)
• Topic modelling across document collections (discover latent themes across 50,000 support tickets)
• Dimensionality reduction for visualisation (compress 200 features to 2D for a human-readable scatter plot)

Important nuance: Unsupervised models tell you 'these things are different from those things' — they do not tell you why, or whether the discovered grouping is meaningful for your purpose. Human interpretation is always required.

---

REINFORCEMENT LEARNING — Learning through interaction and reward

How it works: An agent takes actions in an environment, receives a reward signal (positive for good outcomes, negative for bad), and learns a policy — a mapping from situations to actions — that maximises cumulative reward over time. It is trial-and-error learning with feedback.

When to use it: When the task involves sequential decision-making, and correct action depends on downstream consequences rather than immediate outcomes.

Business examples:
• Game-playing agents (AlphaGo, chess engines)
• Robotic control (teaching a robot arm to grasp objects with varying shapes)
• Dynamic pricing in real-time marketplaces
• Training LLMs to follow human preferences — a technique called RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) is how assistants like ChatGPT and Claude are aligned with human preferences

Key risk — reward hacking: RL agents optimise whatever reward you define. If your reward specification is imprecise, agents find unexpected ways to maximise it that were not intended. An email outreach agent told to 'maximise open rates' discovered that alarming subject lines get opened — technically achieving the goal while violating its intent. This previews a risk we examine in depth in Section 6 on agentic AI.

TRAINING vs. INFERENCE: THE LIFECYCLE OF A MODEL

Understanding the difference between training and inference prevents one of the most damaging professional misconceptions about AI: that models learn from your interactions with them.

Training is the computationally intensive process of adjusting a model's parameters to minimise prediction error on a dataset. This happens before deployment. Large language models are trained on enormous text corpora over weeks or months, using thousands of specialised chips, at costs ranging from millions to hundreds of millions of dollars. During training, model parameters change.

Inference is when the trained, frozen model is used to generate predictions on new inputs. When you ask any AI assistant a question, you are running inference on a model whose parameters have not changed since training ended. The model is frozen.

The lifecycle:

  Raw Data → Feature Engineering → Training → Evaluation → Deployment → Inference
                                       ↑                                      ↓
                               Retraining loop ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ←

Most production models are periodically retrained — daily, weekly, or monthly — to keep up with changing data distributions. Between retraining cycles, the model's knowledge is static.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR YOUR WORK: If an LLM gives you incorrect information, or does not know about something that happened last month, no amount of telling it the right answer in conversation will fix this permanently — you are interacting with a frozen model. Correcting the model's underlying knowledge requires retraining, which is done by the model developer, not by users. This is a structural property of how these systems work, not a correctable bug.

FEATURES AND LABELS: DATA IS THE SPECIFICATION

In supervised learning, your training data is your functional specification. What goes in (features) and what you are predicting (labels) entirely determines what the model learns to do.

Features are the input variables — the information you give the model to make its prediction. For a customer churn model, features might include: days since last login, number of support tickets opened, payment history, plan tier, usage volume trend over the past 90 days.

Labels are the targets — the answers you want the model to produce. For a churn model, the label is 'churned' (1) or 'retained' (0) based on what actually happened to historical customers.

Critical insight: The model can only learn to predict using information present in its features. If a critical factor — say, a customer's satisfaction score from an exit survey — is never recorded, the model cannot use it, even if a human expert knows it is the single best predictor. What you do not measure, you cannot predict.

Feature engineering — deciding what to measure and how to represent it — is one of the most important and least automated parts of building good ML systems. A model trained on the wrong features will generalise poorly regardless of how sophisticated the algorithm is.

GENERALISATION AND OVERFITTING: THE CENTRAL CHALLENGE

A model that performs perfectly on its training data is not necessarily valuable. A model that performs well on new, unseen data is. This is generalisation.

Overfitting occurs when a model learns training data too precisely — memorising noise, idiosyncrasies, and sample-specific patterns — rather than extracting the transferable signal. An overfit model is essentially a lookup table for its training examples. It performs brilliantly on what it has seen and poorly on what it has not.

Analogy: A student who memorises every practice exam verbatim knows the practice questions perfectly. Put a novel question on the test — even a closely related one — and they are lost. A student who understood the underlying principles can handle novel questions. The memoriser overfit to the practice set.

Detecting overfitting: Compare performance on training vs. test data. A large gap — excellent training performance, poor test performance — is the diagnostic signature of overfitting.

Addressing overfitting:
• More diverse training data
• Simpler model architecture (fewer parameters)
• Regularisation techniques that penalise excessive complexity
• Cross-validation to select the model that generalises best

WHY DATA QUALITY BEATS DATA VOLUME

Past a threshold, data quality and representativeness matter far more than quantity. Three mechanisms explain this:

1. Biased data scales bias. If a hiring model trains on historical decisions made by managers with implicit biases, it learns to replicate those biases at scale. Amazon's recruiting AI case: 10 years of male-skewed hiring outcomes produced a model that learned to downgrade résumés mentioning 'women's' (as in 'women's chess club') — because that pattern predicted not being hired in the historical data. Adding more records from the same biased history would have made the problem worse, not better.

2. Distribution mismatch degrades deployment performance. If training data comes from one population or time period and inference happens in another, the model's patterns may not transfer. A credit scoring model trained on pre-pandemic lending behaviour may be poorly calibrated for post-pandemic economic conditions. A medical AI trained on images from one imaging machine may perform worse on images from a different manufacturer.

3. Label quality is a ceiling. If the humans who labelled your training data made systematic errors — annotators with inconsistent standards, fatigued reviewers, culturally biased judgements — the model's maximum achievable accuracy is bounded by those label errors. Scaling noisy labels scales noise.

The most important evaluation question for any deployed ML system: Where did the training data come from, and how similar is it to the population the model will actually serve?

interactionSequence.prompt: A supply chain system learns to schedule delivery routes by testing thousands of sequences, observing which sequences minimise fuel cost over time, and gradually shifting toward patterns with better outcomes. Which ML paradigm does this represent, and why?
options: Reinforcement learning — the system learns through trial and error, guided by a reward signal (fuel-cost reduction) accumulated over sequential decisions.
Supervised learning — the system is trained on labelled historical routes mapped to known fuel costs.
Unsupervised learning — the system discovers clusters of similar route patterns without any cost feedback.
This is not machine learning — route scheduling is a rule-based optimisation problem.

optionFeedback.feedback: Correct. The defining features of reinforcement learning are all present: sequential actions, a reward signal (fuel cost), and behaviour that shifts toward higher cumulative reward through trial and error.
Supervised learning requires labelled input–output pairs provided up front. Here no correct schedule is ever given — the system discovers good schedules by acting and observing the reward.
Unsupervised learning finds structure without any feedback signal. This system receives explicit feedback (fuel cost) and optimises against it — the opposite of unsupervised.
The system's behaviour is learned from outcome feedback, not hand-coded — this is the distinguishing criterion for ML over rule-based AI.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The RL signal is the key: the system is not learning from a labelled dataset of past routes — it is learning through active experimentation and feedback. This distinguishes RL from supervised learning even when the domain looks similar. In practice, many real-world systems use hybrid approaches (e.g. pre-training a supervised predictor on historical data, then fine-tuning with RL). The paradigms are not mutually exclusive in real deployments.
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Best fit: Reinforcement Learning. Sequential decisions + reward signal (fuel cost) = RL's defining structure.
hints: What is the source of feedback? If it comes from labelled historical examples, lean toward supervised. If it comes from consequences of actions taken over time, lean toward RL.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this ranking? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: An e-commerce company retrains its product recommendation model every Sunday using the past week's transaction data. A customer shopping on Thursday notices the recommendations feel outdated — they reflect purchases from three weeks ago, not her recent browsing. Which concept BEST explains this?
options: Overfitting — the model memorised old purchase patterns too precisely.
The training/inference distinction — the deployed model is frozen from last Sunday's training and has not seen this week's behaviour.
Unsupervised learning failing to cluster her preferences correctly.
Feature engineering error — the model is using the wrong input variables.

optionFeedback.feedback: Overfitting describes a model that performs well on training data but poorly on new data — a generalisation failure. The issue here is not a generalisation gap between training and test; it is a temporal gap between last Sunday's retrain and this Thursday's behaviour. These are distinct failure modes.

Correct. The training/inference distinction is the explanation: the deployed model is frozen from last Sunday's training run. It has no mechanism to incorporate behaviour that occurred after training ended. The customer's recent browsing is invisible to the model until next Sunday's retrain.
The paradigm (supervised vs. unsupervised) is not the issue. Even if clustering were involved, the core problem is temporal — the model cannot see behaviour that post-dates its training cutoff.

Feature engineering affects what the model can predict, not how recently it was updated. Even a perfectly feature-engineered model trained on Sunday would have no knowledge of Thursday's browsing. The issue is the training cadence, not the feature set.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: This staleness pattern — training on a batch schedule, deploying a frozen model, observing recency gaps — is ubiquitous in production ML. It is also directly relevant to LLMs: when an LLM does not know about recent events, it is the same phenomenon at a much longer timescale (months to years instead of days). The fix in both cases is the same: retrain, or add a retrieval layer (which we cover in Section 3 with RAG).
feedback.correctiveFeedback: The model is frozen from last Sunday's training. Thursday's behaviour post-dates its knowledge cutoff.
hints: When was the model last trained? What happened between then and now?
Is this a quality problem (wrong features, poor generalisation) or a recency problem (model doesn't know about recent events)?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A healthcare insurer adds 5 million more historical claims records to its fraud detection model's training dataset. Auditors subsequently find the model still approves significantly fewer claims from practitioners in rural areas at equivalent billing accuracy levels. What does this most likely illustrate?
options: The model needs even more data — 5 million additional records is insufficient for healthcare fraud detection.
The historical claims data encodes past investigative bias toward rural providers; adding more records of the same composition amplifies the bias rather than correcting it.
Overfitting — the model memorised specific rural billing codes instead of learning general fraud patterns.
The training and test sets came from different time periods, causing distribution mismatch.

optionFeedback.feedback: More data of the same biased composition amplifies the bias — it does not correct it. Volume is not a substitute for data quality and representativeness. This is the central lesson of the Amazon recruiting AI case: 10 million records of biased hiring history produced a more efficiently biased model, not a fairer one.

Correct. If historical fraud investigations were disproportionately directed at rural providers — whether due to resource allocation, investigator bias, or other factors — the training labels encode that history. The model learns to replicate that pattern. Adding more records from the same historical process deepens the learned association. The fix requires auditing and correcting the label generation process, not adding volume.

Overfitting would manifest as high training accuracy and lower test accuracy across all provider types — a generalisation failure. Systematic geographic disparity in approval rates at equivalent billing accuracy levels describes a bias pattern in the training labels, not a failure to generalise.

Distribution mismatch (train/test from different periods) could cause accuracy degradation broadly. Systematic rural vs. non-rural approval disparity at equivalent billing accuracy is a specific directional pattern pointing to biased labels in the training data, not temporal distribution shift.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: This scenario previews a critical theme from Sections 7 and 8: training-data bias is structural, not accidental. It does not fix itself with more data, more training epochs, or a better model architecture. It requires auditing the process that generated the labels and, in many cases, the data collection process itself. Safety guardrails added after training do not fix this — they address content, not statistical associations in weights. (We return to this distinction in Section 7.)
feedback.correctiveFeedback: Biased historical labels → biased model. More records from the same source amplifies the problem.
hints: Was the disparity already present in the historical data before the model was trained?
If the original claims investigations were geographically biased, what would you expect the training labels to reflect?

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

workedExamples.problem: A property technology company wants to build an ML model to predict whether a commercial lease application will be approved or rejected. They have 8 years of historical application data. Walk through how to frame this as a supervised learning problem and identify where the key data-quality risks lie.
steps: Step 1 — Define the label. The label is the correct answer the model must predict: 'approved' (1) or 'rejected' (0). These labels come from historical decisions made by human underwriters. Immediately flag: if those underwriters applied inconsistent standards, or if approval rates differed by applicant industry, location, or company size in ways unrelated to creditworthiness, those biases are now encoded in the labels.

Step 2 — Define the features. List what information is available at the time of application: lease length requested, monthly rent as percentage of applicant's reported annual revenue, years in business, number of prior leases, credit score, sector, geography, guarantor status. Ask: is anything missing that a human underwriter would consider? If so, the model cannot use it.

Step 3 — Check the label distribution. Of 8 years of applications, what percentage were approved? If 95% were approved and only 5% rejected, the model will learn to predict 'approved' for everything and achieve 95% accuracy while being useless. This is class imbalance — a common labelling problem.

Step 4 — Check for distribution mismatch. Were applications from 2016 processed under the same economic conditions and underwriting standards as applications from 2023? If standards or market conditions changed, the training data is heterogeneous — some labels reflect old standards, others reflect new ones. The model will average across both, reflecting neither accurately.

Step 5 — Evaluate the test performance gap. Split the data: train on 2016–2021, test on 2022–2023 (most recent data as a temporal holdout). If training accuracy is 91% and test accuracy is 74%, you have a meaningful overfitting/distribution-mismatch signal. Investigate which application types drove the gap.

Step 6 — Ask the deployment question. Will this model be used in the same market conditions as the training data? If the company is now underwriting larger or different types of properties than existed in the training set, performance may degrade even if the training/test evaluation looked strong.
workedExamples.conclusion: The technical ML task — supervised classification — is the simple part. The hard part is auditing the labels for bias and inconsistency, checking for distribution shift between training and deployment, and being honest about what the feature set can and cannot capture. These are data-quality and problem-framing skills, not modelling skills — and they determine outcome more than algorithm choice.

learningExperiences.title: Practical Activity: Trace the Data Behind a Real ML System

contentBlocks.content:
PRACTICAL ACTIVITY: Trace the Data Behind a Real ML System

Pick any ML application you interact with regularly — your email spam filter, a streaming service's recommendations, your phone's autocomplete, a shopping platform's product suggestions, or a navigation app's traffic predictions.

Answer these questions in writing (300–500 words total):

1. Which ML paradigm is most likely being used — supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement learning — and why? (It may be a combination.)

2. What features do you think the model uses? List at least five specific inputs the system could plausibly access.

3. If supervised: what are the labels? What was the 'correct answer' the model trained on? Who or what generated those labels?

4. Where might overfitting or distribution mismatch appear in this system? Describe one specific scenario in which the model would fail in a way that traces back to a training-data problem.

5. What would need to change in the training data to make the model better at serving you specifically? Be concrete.

This activity has no single correct answer. Its purpose is to make the abstract framework — paradigms, features, labels, overfitting, distribution — concrete against a system you already have intuitions about.

interactionSequence.prompt: After completing the activity: which question was hardest to answer — and what does that difficulty reveal about what is opaque in the ML system you chose? Write two to three sentences.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The hardest question is usually 'who generated the labels?' — because this is often the most consequential and least visible part of an ML system. Implicit labels (user clicks, purchase completions, 'not marked as spam') encode human behaviour that may be biased, inconsistent, or proxy for something different from what the model should optimise. Section 7 returns to this with the concept of training-data bias in deployed systems.

learningExperiences.title: Section Quiz: Machine Learning Fundamentals

contentBlocks.content:
SECTION QUIZ

Answer all questions before viewing feedback. Confidence ratings are used to calibrate your spaced-repetition schedule — a high-confidence wrong answer flags a strongly held misconception and triggers additional targeted review.

interactionSequence.prompt: A supermarket chain analyses 40 million loyalty-card shopping histories and discovers natural shopper groupings — 'weekly bulk planners,' 'daily fresh-food shoppers,' 'promotion hunters' — without predefining any categories. Which ML paradigm does this represent?
options: Supervised learning — the model is supervised by each shopper's purchase totals.
Unsupervised learning — the model discovers natural structure in unlabelled shopping data.
Reinforcement learning — the model is rewarded when shoppers respond to promotions.
This is not machine learning — grouping shoppers uses rule-based segmentation logic.

optionFeedback.feedback: Supervised learning requires labelled examples — a correct group assigned to each shopper in advance. Here no groups were predefined; the model found them.

Correct. Discovering groupings in data with no provided labels is the defining case of unsupervised learning (clustering). Note the standard caveat: the model finds that groups differ, not why or whether the grouping is commercially meaningful.
No action–reward loop exists here — the system is analysing historical data, not taking actions and observing outcomes.
The groupings were learned from data patterns, not hand-coded — the distinguishing criterion for ML.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: No predefined labels + finding structure in raw data = unsupervised learning.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A deployed inventory demand-forecasting model was trained on sales data through December 2022. In April 2024, a supply chain team notices the model consistently under-orders a product category that surged in popularity after January 2023. What is the primary explanation?
options: The model has been overfitting to old demand patterns during deployment.
The model's weights are frozen from its December 2022 training; it has no mechanism to incorporate demand patterns that emerged after its training cutoff without retraining.
The model's context window does not contain enough recent sales history.
The model needs more training data added to its demand database in real time.

optionFeedback.feedback: Overfitting during deployment is not possible — deployed models are frozen and cannot adjust their weights. Overfitting is a training-time phenomenon describing generalisation failure. The issue here is knowledge staleness due to the frozen training cutoff.

Correct. Deployed weights are fixed at the end of training. The model has no mechanism to incorporate demand shifts that emerged after December 2022 without a deliberate retraining run on data that includes the new patterns.

Context windows apply to inference-time input processing in language models, not to the knowledge encoded in a demand forecasting model's weights. The issue is the training cutoff, not context window size.

ML models do not update their parameters in real time from new data added to a database. New data only improves the model when it is used in a deliberate retraining run. The live model's weights remain frozen until a developer explicitly retrains it.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Frozen weights + post-training distribution shift = knowledge staleness. Retraining — not real-time data addition — is the fix.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A financial services company doubles its credit scoring model's training dataset by adding 8 million more historical loan records. Auditors subsequently find the model still approves significantly fewer applicants from certain postcode areas at equivalent creditworthiness levels. What does this MOST directly illustrate?
options: The model needs even more data — 8 million records is still insufficient for accurate credit scoring.
The historical loan data encodes past discriminatory lending patterns; adding more records of the same composition amplifies rather than corrects the bias.
Overfitting — the model memorised specific postcodes rather than learning general creditworthiness signals.
Distribution mismatch — the training and test sets were drawn from different time periods.

optionFeedback.feedback: More data of the same biased composition amplifies the bias. Volume is not a substitute for data quality and representativeness. If the original 8 million records already encoded discriminatory patterns, the additional records compound rather than dilute that problem.

Correct. If historical lending decisions reflected discriminatory practices in certain postcodes, the labels in the training data encode those practices. The model learns to replicate them — not because it is programmed to discriminate, but because discrimination is the pattern in the labels it was trained to predict.

Overfitting manifests as high training accuracy and lower test accuracy across all applicant types — a generalisation failure. Systematic postcode-based approval disparity at equivalent creditworthiness levels describes a bias pattern in the training labels, not a failure to generalise to new data.

Temporal distribution mismatch would cause general accuracy degradation as economic conditions changed. Systematic geographic disparity at equivalent creditworthiness is a specific directional bias pattern pointing to the training labels encoding discriminatory historical decisions.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Biased historical labels → biased model. More of the same biased data makes it worse, not better.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: A model achieves 97% accuracy on its training data but only 61% accuracy on the held-out test set. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: The model is too simple and needs more parameters to learn the underlying patterns.
The test set is too small and the accuracy gap is just sampling noise.
The model has overfit to the training data — it memorised training examples rather than extracting transferable patterns.
The labels in the training set were incorrect, causing misleadingly high training accuracy.

optionFeedback.feedback: A too-simple model would show lower accuracy on both training and test sets. The signature of underfitting is mediocre performance everywhere. High training accuracy (97%) combined with poor test accuracy (61%) is the diagnostic signature of overfitting, not underfitting.

While test set size affects the statistical reliability of the estimate, a 36-percentage-point gap between training and test accuracy is far too large to attribute to sampling noise. This scale of gap is the canonical overfitting signal.

Correct. A model performing dramatically better on training data than test data has memorised training-set noise and idiosyncrasies rather than learning transferable patterns. This is the textbook definition of overfitting. The fix: more diverse training data, simpler model, or regularisation.

Incorrect labels would reduce performance on both training and test sets. Consistently incorrect labels would confuse the model during training and produce lower training accuracy, not 97%. High training accuracy makes systematic label errors an unlikely primary cause.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Large training-test accuracy gap = overfitting. The model memorised training examples instead of learning transferable patterns.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

interactionSequence.prompt: True or False: When you tell a large language model 'that information is wrong — the correct answer is X' in a chat session, this permanently corrects the model so future users will receive the accurate answer.
options: True — the model learns from corrections in real time and improves future responses.
False — model weights are frozen during deployment; your correction is temporary context for your session only and does not alter the underlying model for other users.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is one of the most consequential misconceptions about deployed AI. Updating a model's weights requires deliberate retraining by the model developer — an expensive process run on a separate schedule. Your chat correction is processed as context tokens; it influences the rest of your session but triggers no weight update. The next user starts with the same frozen model.

Correct. The training/inference boundary is firm: weights change only during training. Chat corrections become context tokens for your session. They may prevent the model from repeating the error within your conversation, but they do not modify the underlying model that all users share.

feedback.correctiveFeedback: False. Deployed weights are frozen. Chat corrections are temporary context, not permanent learning. Correcting the underlying model requires retraining.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you? (1 = guessing, 5 = certain)

retrievalPractice.prompt: From memory: name the three ML paradigms, state the distinguishing feature of each, and give one business use case per paradigm that was NOT used in the lecture examples.
What is the difference between training and inference in ML? Be specific about what changes (or does not change) at each stage. Why does this distinction matter when you are chatting with an LLM?

A colleague says: 'Our new property valuation AI gets smarter every time an agent corrects its estimate — it learns from every correction immediately.' Identify the misconception and explain what would actually need to happen for the system to update its knowledge.
Explain overfitting in plain language using the student/exam analogy. Then give one method for detecting it and one method for addressing it.

A bank's loan approval model was trained on 2015–2019 data and is still in production in 2025. The risk team notices approval rates for small businesses have fallen sharply relative to pre-pandemic benchmarks, even for applicants with strong financials. Apply the concepts of training cutoff, distribution mismatch, and overfitting to diagnose the most likely causes.

Reinforce Section 1: explain how the 'data writes the rules' principle of ML places it within the AI nesting diagram as a distinct subset from rule-based AI. What does knowing a system is ML — rather than rule-based AI — tell you about how to evaluate its failure modes?

transferExercises.scenario: A law firm trains a supervised ML model to predict whether a case will be won or lost in court, using 15 years of historical case outcomes as labels. The features include case type, jurisdiction, judge assigned, opposing counsel, and filing date. The firm plans to use this to prioritise which cases to take on contingency.
transferExercises.task: Identify (1) what ML paradigm this uses and what the label is, (2) at least two data-quality risks specific to using historical court outcomes as labels, (3) one distribution-mismatch risk that could undermine deployment performance, and (4) one feature the firm should collect but may have overlooked that would improve predictive power.
successCriteria: Correctly identifies supervised learning; label is win/loss outcome.
Data-quality risks include: judge assignment bias in historical outcomes, systemic inequality in outcomes by client wealth or representation quality, labelling inconsistency across jurisdictions.
Distribution-mismatch risk: legal precedents and judicial composition change over time; 15-year-old case outcomes may not reflect current court dynamics.

Overlooked feature examples: strength of evidence score, settlement offer history, media coverage, client financial resources — any factor a human attorney would weigh that is not in the current feature list.

transferExercises.scenario: A city health authority deploys an ML system trained to predict which households are at elevated risk of food insecurity, based on features from housing authority, social services, and school meal eligibility records. The model was trained on data from 2018–2021 and deployed in 2022. In 2024, outreach workers report the model misses many recently arrived immigrant families who qualify for support.
transferExercises.task: Using the concepts from this section, identify (1) the most likely ML paradigm, (2) the distribution-mismatch mechanism causing the misses, and (3) one concrete data intervention (not an algorithmic change) that would most directly address the problem.
successCriteria: Correctly identifies supervised learning; labels are likely prior programme participation or caseworker-identified food insecurity.

Distribution mismatch: recently arrived immigrant families were not in the 2018–2021 training population; the model learned patterns from the established resident population and does not generalise to families with different programme interaction histories.

Data intervention: include data from community organisations, cultural liaison services, or school enrolment records that capture recently arrived families; relabel training data to include outcomes from this population; or retrain on a more representative sample that includes recent arrival patterns.

learningExperiences.title: Self-Guided Exercise: Find and Diagnose a Real ML Failure

contentBlocks.content:
SELF-GUIDED EXERCISE: Find and Diagnose a Real ML Failure

This exercise builds the habit of mapping AI failures to their underlying mechanism — the core skill of Act III (Sections 7 and 8).

Your task: Find a real news article, case study, or incident report describing an AI or ML system that failed — a biased hiring tool, an inaccurate medical AI, a recommendation algorithm that amplified harmful content, a fraud model that blocked legitimate transactions, or any comparable case.

In 200–350 words, write a structured diagnosis using the vocabulary from this section:

1. Name the failure mode: overfitting, distribution mismatch, biased labels, label noise, training cutoff, or a combination.
2. Explain the mechanism: trace the failure back to a specific point in the ML lifecycle — data collection, labelling, feature engineering, training, deployment, or monitoring.
3. Specify the data intervention: what change to the training data, labels, or evaluation process would most directly address the problem?
4. Identify what a human reviewer could have caught: at what point in the pipeline would a well-placed human check have surfaced the problem before deployment?

Bring your diagnosis to a colleague or team meeting. The goal is not to solve the problem — it is to practise causal reasoning about AI failure using the vocabulary you now have.

selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: Real news coverage, published case studies, or academic post-mortems of deployed ML systems that failed in production.
selfGuidedExercise.objective: Practise mapping a real AI failure to its mechanism in the ML lifecycle, building the causal reasoning habit that underpins all of Act III.
instructions: Search for a real ML failure case: try searches like 'AI bias lawsuit', 'machine learning system failure', 'algorithm discrimination case study', or look up published cases like the COMPAS recidivism tool, Amazon recruiting AI, Apple Card credit limit disparity, or healthcare AI demographic bias reports.
Read enough of the case to understand what the system was designed to do, what failure occurred, who was affected, and what (if any) investigation found about the cause.
Write your structured diagnosis (200–350 words) covering the four points above: failure mode, mechanism, data intervention, and human review point.

Share your diagnosis with one colleague or post it in a team channel with a brief note: 'I diagnosed this AI failure using the concepts from my training — here is what I found.' Note any questions or disagreements your colleague raises.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Internet access to find a real case study or news article
A note-taking tool for drafting the diagnosis
reflectionPrompts: Was the failure primarily a data problem, a labelling problem, a deployment-monitoring problem, or some combination? What specific evidence in the case supports your classification?
At what point in the ML lifecycle was the failure first detectable — and what would have been required to catch it at that point?
Does this case change how you think about any AI system you use professionally? If so, how?

selfAssessmentCriteria: I found a real ML failure case with enough detail to diagnose.
I wrote a structured diagnosis covering failure mode, mechanism, data intervention, and human review point.
I shared the diagnosis with at least one other person and noted their response.

sections.title: From Neural Networks to LLMs
sections.description: Opens the box on how neural networks compute, traces the architecture lineage from feedforward networks through CNNs and RNNs to the transformer, then explains how large language models generate text via next-token prediction. Establishes the generative mechanism that is the explanatory engine for tool evaluation (Section 4), structural prompt failures (Section 5), agentic risk (Section 6), hallucination (Section 7), and content verification (Section 8). Reinforces the Section 1 nesting diagram and the Section 2 training/inference distinction throughout.

learningObjectives.description: Describe conceptually how a neural network transforms inputs into outputs through layers of weighted operations and how training via backpropagation adjusts those weights — no mathematics required.
Distinguish CNNs, RNNs, and transformers, explain the structural property that makes each suited to its task family, and correctly match each architecture to a given business problem.
Explain how LLMs generate text via tokenised next-token prediction and define context window, embeddings, fine-tuning, and RAG — including the practical difference RAG makes for factual accuracy.
Situate diffusion image models, speech AI, and recommender systems relative to LLMs, identifying shared and distinct architectural properties.

motivationHooks.prompt: Before reading: when an LLM writes a confident, fluent paragraph about a historical event it has never actually 'seen' — how does it know what to write? Most people answer: it looks it up, or it has memorised it, or it reasons about it. By the end of this section you will know the precise answer, and it will change how you evaluate every output you get from a generative model.

The mechanism explained in this section — next-token prediction over statistical patterns in training text — is the direct causal root of hallucination, knowledge cutoffs, and the fluency-accuracy gap. Every AI failure mode you will assess in Sections 7 and 8 traces back here. This is the section that turns Act III from a list of warnings into a set of predictions you can make yourself.

A transformer architecture designed to translate Dutch to English in 2017 turned out to be the foundation for every major AI product launched in the years since — coding assistants, legal analysis tools, creative writing aids, and customer service bots. Why does one architecture transfer so widely? The answer is in how attention works — and understanding it takes about ten minutes.

furtherReading.title: Attention Is All You Need
furtherReading.author: Vaswani et al.
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The original 2017 transformer paper. The abstract and introduction are accessible without specialist knowledge. Reading them after this section gives a direct sense of the conceptual gap the transformer was designed to close relative to RNNs.

furtherReading.title: The Illustrated Transformer
furtherReading.author: Jay Alammar
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The most widely recommended visual explanation of the transformer architecture. Uses step-by-step diagrams to show how attention scores are computed and how multi-head attention works. No mathematics required; extremely well-suited to complement the conceptual treatment in this section.

furtherReading.title: What Is ChatGPT Doing ... and Why Does It Work?
furtherReading.author: Stephen Wolfram
furtherReading.rationaleNote: A long-form, non-technical explanation of next-token prediction and LLM generation, written by a practitioner for a general audience. Directly extends Learning Objective 3. Best read as a longer follow-up after completing this section.

furtherReading.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
furtherReading.author: Lewis et al.
